choices: x equals x_0 plus v_0 t plus half a t squared
questions.explanation: Velocity is a vector: v = Delta x / Delta t (m/s) with both magnitude and direction. Galileo first distinguished it from instantaneous speed when studying inclined-plane motion in the 1600s. On AP Physics 1 FRQs (Science Practice 2: Mathematical Routines), students must use signed velocities to predict motion direction and slope position-time graphs to extract v at an instant. Average velocity uses displacement, not path length — a key conceptual distinction tested in modeling questions where students argue why two students traveling the same distance can have different average velocities.

choices: x equals x_0 plus v_0 t plus half a t squared
questions.explanation: Acceleration is a vector: a = Delta v / Delta t (m/s^2). Galileo's free-fall experiments established that all objects near Earth accelerate at g = 9.8 m/s^2 regardless of mass — a foundational AP claim. Sign matters: a negative a with positive v means slowing down, while negative a with negative v means speeding up in the negative direction. AP FRQs (Science Practice 4: Data Analysis) often hand students a v-t graph and ask them to find acceleration from the slope and justify whether the object speeds up or slows down.

choices: v equals v_0 plus a t
x equals x_0 plus v_0 t plus half a t squared
questions.correctText: x equals x_0 plus v_0 t plus half a t squared
questions.explanation: x = x_0 + v_0 t + (1/2) a t^2 (meters), where x_0 is initial position (m), v_0 initial velocity (m/s), a constant acceleration (m/s^2), t time (s). Derived from Galileo's mean-speed theorem and standard in the AP equation table. On Paragraph-Length-Argument FRQs (Science Practice 7), students apply this to a dropped ball or a car braking and explain why the quadratic term dominates at large t. A symmetry check: at t = 0 it reduces to x_0, confirming initial conditions.

choices: v equals v_0 plus a t
v squared equals v_0 squared plus 2 a delta x
questions.correctText: v equals v_0 plus a t
questions.explanation: v = v_0 + a t (m/s). This linear equation is the derivative of x(t) and the antiderivative of the constant a. Newton implicitly used it in his Principia (1687); it appears on the AP equation sheet. AP FRQs commonly pair it with v^2 = v_0^2 + 2 a Delta x to solve two-stage problems (a rocket accelerates, then coasts) under Science Practice 5: Calculations. Always plot v versus t and verify: a positive slope of v(t) equals a, while the t-intercept gives the moment v reverses sign.

choices: v equals v_0 plus a t
v squared equals v_0 squared plus 2 a delta x
questions.correctText: v squared equals v_0 squared plus 2 a delta x
questions.explanation: v^2 = v_0^2 + 2 a Delta x (m^2/s^2) — the time-independent kinematic equation. Algebraically derived by eliminating t between x(t) and v(t). Indispensable on AP FRQs (Science Practice 5) when timing data is missing — for example, a stopping-distance problem where students know v_0, v_f = 0, and Delta x but not t. Limiting case check: when a = 0, v^2 = v_0^2, recovering constant velocity. Useful for projectile peak-height calculations (set v_y = 0 to find max height).

questions.prompt: Object given initial velocity then only gravity.
choices: v_0 squared sin 2 theta over g
questions.explanation: A projectile undergoes 2D motion with the ONLY force being gravity (air resistance neglected on AP Physics 1). Galileo decomposed projectile motion into independent horizontal (constant v) and vertical (constant a = -g) components — a Science Practice 1 modeling skill. On AP FRQs (Science Practice 6: Argumentation), students must explicitly justify the no-air-resistance assumption before applying constant-acceleration kinematics separately to each axis, then combine for range and time of flight using x = v_{0x} t and y = v_{0y} t - (1/2) g t^2.

choices: v_0 squared sin 2 theta over g
questions.explanation: g = 9.8 m/s^2 (often rounded to 10 for AP estimation) acts downward on any projectile near Earth's surface, independent of mass or shape. Galileo's Leaning Tower experiment (apocryphal but historically attributed to him) established this; Newton's law of universal gravitation later derived g = G M_E / R_E^2. AP FRQ tip: always sketch a coordinate system with up as positive so a = -9.8 m/s^2 (Science Practice 1: Modeling). On Atwood and incline problems, g appears as the source of weight mg.

v_x = v_0 cos(theta) stays constant throughout flight because no horizontal force acts (air resistance ignored). This is Galileo's independence-of-motion principle and the conceptual heart of projectile FRQs. AP graders look for explicit reasoning (Science Practice 6) that since F_net,x = 0, Newton's second law gives a_x = 0, so v_x is unchanged. Therefore horizontal range is simply x = v_x * t_flight. A common student error: assuming v_x decreases — graders mark this as a violation of Newton's first law.

choices: v_0 squared sin 2 theta over g
questions.explanation: At the peak of projectile flight v_y = 0 m/s, but the projectile is NOT in equilibrium — gravity still acts, so a_y = -g and the projectile continues to accelerate downward. AP FRQ classic trap (Science Practice 2): students must distinguish v = 0 from a = 0. Using v_y = v_{0y} - g t with v_y = 0 gives t_peak = v_{0y}/g; symmetry then implies t_flight = 2 t_peak for level ground. Peak height follows from v_y^2 = v_{0y}^2 - 2 g h_peak with v_y = 0.

choices: v_0 squared sin 2 theta over g
questions.correctText: v_0 squared sin 2 theta over g
questions.explanation: Range R = v_0^2 sin(2 theta) / g (meters), valid only for level ground (launch height = landing height). Derived by combining t_flight = 2 v_0 sin(theta) / g with x = v_0 cos(theta) * t_flight, then applying the identity 2 sin cos = sin(2 theta). Maximum range occurs at theta = 45 degrees (sin 90 = 1). AP FRQs (Science Practice 5: Calculations) frequently ask students to compare ranges at complementary angles (15 and 75 degrees give equal range) or to find launch angle from a known range — a quadratic in theta.

A vector has both magnitude and direction. Position, velocity, acceleration, force, momentum, and torque are all vectors in AP Physics 1. Vector notation (bold or arrow) and component decomposition is a Science Practice 1 modeling skill — every free-body diagram on the AP exam requires resolving vectors into x and y components. The 19th-century work of Hamilton (quaternions) and Gibbs/Heaviside (modern vector algebra) formalized what physicists had used informally since Newton. Vector addition is head-to-tail or component-wise: A_x + B_x = R_x.

A scalar has magnitude but no direction. Time, mass, distance, speed, energy, work, power, and temperature are scalars. AP FRQ trap (Science Practice 6): students must distinguish speed (scalar) from velocity (vector) — a question may state 'the speed at the top of the loop is 5 m/s' and require students to acknowledge that direction information is missing for a complete kinematic description. Scalars combine by simple arithmetic (5 J + 3 J = 8 J), unlike vectors which require component addition or the parallelogram rule.

Components: A_x = A cos(theta), A_y = A sin(theta), where theta is measured from the +x axis. Cartesian decomposition is the workhorse of every AP Physics 1 FRQ — inclined planes (decompose mg into parallel and perpendicular components), projectile motion (decompose v_0), and tension problems all require it. Science Practice 1 (Modeling) and Science Practice 5 (Calculations) demand that students annotate diagrams with components and use Pythagoras (|A| = sqrt(A_x^2 + A_y^2)) plus arctan(A_y / A_x) to recover the magnitude and angle of the resultant.

Head-to-tail (tip-to-tail) method: place the tail of vector B at the head of vector A; the resultant R points from tail of A to head of B. Equivalent to the parallelogram rule. While AP Physics 1 emphasizes algebraic component addition for FRQ calculations (Science Practice 5), graphical methods remain essential for Science Practice 1: Modeling — quickly sketching net force from a free-body diagram or finding the direction of a momentum change Delta p = p_f - p_i. Vector subtraction A - B is performed by adding -B (reversed) head-to-tail with A.

Magnitude |A| = sqrt(A_x^2 + A_y^2) (units match the vector's units). Pythagorean theorem applied to perpendicular components. On AP Physics 1 FRQs (Science Practice 5: Calculations), magnitude appears when students sum forces in 2D — for example, finding the net force on a hanging sign supported by two cables, where the cable tensions have both horizontal and vertical components that must be vector-summed and then equated to mg. The direction comes from theta = arctan(A_y / A_x) with quadrant awareness.

Slope of x(t) gives v(t): v = dx/dt or geometrically rise-over-run on a position-time graph. A horizontal line means v = 0 (object at rest); a straight inclined line means constant v; a curving line means changing v (i.e., acceleration). AP FRQs (Science Practice 4: Data Analysis) routinely give students a position-time graph and require them to extract instantaneous velocity at specific points by drawing tangent lines, then justify (Science Practice 6) whether the object is speeding up or slowing down based on slope steepness changes.

Slope of v(t) gives a(t): a = dv/dt. A horizontal v-t line means a = 0 (constant velocity); an inclined v-t line means constant a; a curving v-t line means changing a (jerk, beyond AP). When v and a have the same sign the object speeds up; when they have opposite signs it slows down. AP FRQ classic (Science Practice 4): handed a piecewise-linear v-t graph, students compute acceleration on each segment and must explicitly justify direction of motion using sign conventions defined on a coordinate axis.

Area under v(t) gives displacement Delta x (meters). Geometrically: count signed area — area below the t-axis is negative displacement (motion in the -x direction). For constant velocity v over time t, area = v * t = Delta x. For constant acceleration starting from rest, area is a triangle: Delta x = (1/2) v_max t. AP FRQs (Science Practice 4) use trapezoid and triangle decomposition to integrate non-trivial v-t graphs, and students must show area computations with units to receive full credit (Science Practice 5).

Area under a(t) gives Delta v (m/s). For constant acceleration over time t: Delta v = a * t (the basic kinematic relation). The hierarchy x → v → a connects by slope (going down: x slope = v, v slope = a) and by area (going up: a area = Delta v, v area = Delta x). AP FRQ (Science Practice 2: Mathematical Routines) testing this chain expects students to read off slope and area values from graphs consistently and combine them — common in collision and rocket-thrust problems where a is piecewise constant.

Slope sign on a position-time graph reveals direction of motion relative to a chosen coordinate axis: positive slope = +x direction, negative slope = -x direction, zero slope = at rest. Same logic applies to v-t slope giving acceleration direction. AP FRQ (Science Practice 6: Argumentation) often asks students to write a paragraph-length argument explaining whether a cart moving along a track is moving toward or away from a sensor based purely on graph slope — students must explicitly reference the coordinate convention they defined.

A reference frame is a coordinate system attached to an observer used to measure positions, velocities, and accelerations. Newton assumed an absolute frame in the Principia (1687); Galileo's relativity principle held that laws of motion are identical in all inertial frames. AP Physics 1 stays strictly within Galilean (non-relativistic) relativity. On FRQs (Science Practice 1: Modeling) students must declare the chosen reference frame at the start — typically the ground frame — and stick with it consistently, since velocities, displacements, and even forces can have different signs in different frames.

An inertial frame is one in which Newton's first law (inertia) holds: an object with no net force moves at constant velocity. Equivalently, any frame moving at constant velocity relative to a known inertial frame is also inertial. Newton's laws as written apply only in inertial frames; in accelerating frames, pseudo-forces (centrifugal, Coriolis) must be added — but AP Physics 1 avoids non-inertial frames. AP FRQs (Science Practice 6) sometimes ask students to argue why a frame attached to an accelerating elevator is not inertial — because objects appear to accelerate without a real force.

The rest frame of an object is the inertial frame in which that object's velocity is zero. Every observer is at rest in their own rest frame by definition. Galilean transformations relate observations between frames: if frame S' moves at velocity u relative to frame S, then v_{in S} = v_{in S'} + u for any object. AP Physics 1 FRQs (Science Practice 2: Mathematical Routines) use this to handle 'velocity of plane relative to ground' problems — students must vector-add the plane-relative-to-air velocity and the air-relative-to-ground (wind) velocity.

questions.prompt: Velocity of A relative to B.
questions.explanation: Relative velocity v_{A/B} = v_A - v_B (m/s), where both v_A and v_B are measured in a common (typically ground) frame. The Galilean transformation. AP FRQ scenarios (Science Practice 5: Calculations): a boat crossing a river — boat-relative-to-water velocity vector plus water-relative-to-ground (current) velocity gives boat-relative-to-ground; students must determine downstream drift and crossing time. Sign convention is critical: v_{A/B} = -v_{B/A}. Students must label diagrams with both vectors explicitly.

Galilean velocity addition: v_{A/C} = v_{A/B} + v_{B/C}, where the middle subscripts must match in chain fashion. Operations are head-to-tail vector additions (or component sums). AP Physics 1 stays non-relativistic, so velocities simply add — Einstein's velocity-addition formula is reserved for AP Physics 2 modern physics. On river-crossing or airplane-with-wind FRQs (Science Practice 5), students must draw the velocity-vector triangle, label all three vectors with both subscripts, and decompose into components to find magnitude and heading.

Newton's first law (1687): an object at rest stays at rest, and an object in motion stays in motion with constant velocity, unless acted on by a net external force. The property quantified is inertia (mass). Galileo's inclined-plane experiments first articulated this principle; Newton elevated it to a law in Principia Mathematica. AP FRQs (Science Practice 1: Modeling) require students to identify inertial frames before applying any Newtonian analysis, and to recognize that an object with zero net force is in equilibrium even if moving (constant v) — a common conceptual trap on multiple-choice items.

F_net = m a (newtons = kg * m/s^2). Newton's second law from Principia (1687) — the central equation of mechanics. F_net is the vector sum of ALL external forces; m is inertial mass; a is the resulting acceleration. AP FRQs (Science Practice 5: Calculations) consistently require students to draw a free-body diagram (Science Practice 1: Modeling), sum forces along each axis, and apply F_net,x = m a_x and F_net,y = m a_y separately. On the AP equation table. Limiting case: if F_net = 0 then a = 0, recovering Newton's first law as a special case.

Newton's third law (1687): for every action force F_{AB}, there is an equal-magnitude opposite-direction reaction force F_{BA} = -F_{AB}, exerted on a different object. AP FRQ classic trap (Science Practice 6: Argumentation): students must explicitly name the partner of each action force AND identify which body exerts it on which body. The two forces never cancel in a free-body diagram because they act on different objects. Earth pulls a falling ball downward with mg; the ball pulls Earth upward with mg — but Earth's mass is so large its acceleration is undetectable.

Momentum p = m v (kg * m/s) is a vector. Newton actually wrote his second law in momentum form: F_net = dp/dt — the rate of change of momentum. For constant mass this reduces to F = m a. Momentum is conserved in isolated systems (no external force), which underlies all AP Physics 1 collision FRQs (Science Practice 5). On the AP equation sheet. Students writing paragraph-length arguments (Science Practice 7) must explicitly invoke 'isolated system' or 'F_ext = 0' as justification for using conservation of momentum to bridge before-and-after states.

questions.prompt: Net force equals rate of momentum change.
questions.explanation: F_net = dp/dt — the original form of Newton's second law as Newton wrote it. For variable mass (e.g., rocket expelling propellant) F = m a is incomplete; F = dp/dt is the general form. AP Physics 1 FRQs (Science Practice 7: Paragraph-Length Argument) often invoke this form for impulse analyses: integrating F dt over the contact time gives Delta p = J (impulse). On a rocket FRQ, students may need to recognize that mass changes — at AP level this is usually treated by computing average force = Delta p / Delta t rather than calculus.

Friction is a contact force parallel to surfaces, opposing relative motion (kinetic) or impending motion (static). Microscopically caused by surface asperities and adhesion at the contact patches. Coulomb (yes, the same Coulomb of electrostatics) first systematized friction laws in 1785. AP FRQs (Science Practice 1: Modeling) require explicit friction force vectors on free-body diagrams, always directed opposite to motion or impending motion. Treated as a single resultant force in AP Physics 1, not microscopically. Friction always reduces kinetic energy by generating thermal energy.

Static friction f_s adjusts up to a maximum f_{s,max} = mu_s N (newtons), where mu_s is the coefficient of static friction (dimensionless) and N is normal force (N). Static friction is NOT a fixed value — it equals whatever applied force is needed to keep the object at rest, up to the ceiling. AP FRQ classic (Science Practice 6): a box on an incline, theta increasing — students must argue that f_s = mg sin(theta) until tan(theta) = mu_s, after which the box slides and kinetic friction takes over. Distinguish static (mu_s) and kinetic (mu_k); mu_s > mu_k usually.

Kinetic friction f_k = mu_k N (newtons) — constant magnitude (to AP-level approximation) once the object slides, directed opposite to velocity. mu_k is the coefficient of kinetic friction, dimensionless. AP FRQs (Science Practice 5: Calculations) frequently combine friction with Newton's second law on inclines: ma = mg sin(theta) - mu_k mg cos(theta) gives a = g(sin(theta) - mu_k cos(theta)). Limiting check: if mu_k = 0 (frictionless), a = g sin(theta), the classic frictionless-incline result. The work-energy theorem with friction: W_friction = -f_k * d (always negative, generates heat).

f = mu N (newtons) — the basic friction relation, with mu either mu_s (max static) or mu_k (kinetic). Crucially: friction depends on the NORMAL force, not on the contact area or weight directly. On an incline, N = mg cos(theta) — not mg — a frequent AP error. On the AP equation sheet. Coulomb established the dependence on N empirically. AP FRQs (Science Practice 5) often require computing N on inclines, in elevators (N = m(g + a)), or with applied vertical forces (a downward push increases N and thus friction).

mu (μ) is the dimensionless coefficient of friction. Typical values: mu_s ≈ 0.6 (rubber on dry concrete), mu_k ≈ 0.4 (steel on steel). AP FRQ experimental-design questions (Science Practice 4: Experimental Design) often ask students to design a procedure to measure mu_s by slowly tilting an incline until the block slips, then computing mu_s = tan(theta_critical). Or to measure mu_k by recording the deceleration of a sliding block: a = -mu_k g, so mu_k = |a|/g. Students must specify what to measure, how, and how to compute uncertainty.

A free-body diagram (FBD) isolates a single object as a dot or box and shows every external force as an arrow originating at the object, labeled with its source (e.g., F_g, F_N, T, f). AP FRQs (Science Practice 1: Modeling) explicitly require a labeled FBD with no extraneous forces (no centripetal pseudo-force, no 'normal reaction of itself'). FBD then drives the application of Newton's second law in vector components. Common AP grading rubric: each missing or extra force costs a point. Internal forces (springs inside the object, atomic forces) never appear.

Weight F_g = m g (newtons), always directed downward (toward Earth's center). Distinguish from mass m (kg, scalar). Newton's law of universal gravitation: F_g = G m M_E / r^2, which near Earth's surface simplifies to mg with g = 9.8 m/s^2. AP FRQs (Science Practice 6: Argumentation) commonly ask students to explain why apparent weight (normal force reading on a scale) differs from true weight in an accelerating elevator — N = m(g + a) when upward-accelerating, N = m(g - a) when downward-accelerating, and N = 0 in free fall (apparent weightlessness).

Normal force N (newtons) is the contact force perpendicular to the surfaces, preventing interpenetration. On level ground with no vertical applied force: N = mg. On an incline: N = mg cos(theta). In an elevator accelerating upward with a: N = m(g + a). Normal force adjusts to whatever value is needed to satisfy the no-penetration constraint — it is NOT a fixed quantity. AP FRQs (Science Practice 5: Calculations) chain N with friction (f = mu N) and circular motion (N supplies centripetal force on banked turns and the bottom of vertical loops).

choices: v_0 squared sin 2 theta over g
questions.explanation: Tension T (newtons) is the pulling force transmitted along a string, rope, or wire, directed along the rope away from the object. AP Physics 1 idealization: ropes are massless and inextensible, so tension has the same magnitude at every point along the rope (no mass means no net force needed to accelerate the rope itself). On Atwood machines (Science Practice 5: Calculations), the rope passing over a frictionless massless pulley keeps T equal on both sides. If the rope has appreciable mass or the pulley has rotational inertia, tensions differ — but those scenarios appear in Unit 5 rotational dynamics.

Net force F_net = vector sum of all external forces (N). By Newton's second law, F_net = m a. AP FRQs (Science Practice 1: Modeling) require students to compute net force component-by-component: F_{net,x} = sum F_x, F_{net,y} = sum F_y. Then a_x = F_{net,x} / m and a_y = F_{net,y} / m. If F_net = 0 the object is in translational equilibrium (constant velocity or rest). Crucial AP distinction (Science Practice 6): equilibrium does not mean at rest; it means a = 0. A satellite in orbit or a car at terminal velocity satisfies this.

questions.prompt: Surface angled with respect to horizontal.
questions.explanation: An inclined plane is a flat surface angled at theta to the horizontal. Galileo used inclined planes to slow gravitational acceleration enough to measure motion with water-clocks, indirectly establishing g. AP FRQs (Science Practice 1: Modeling) require setting up tilted coordinates with x along the incline (positive = down-slope) and y perpendicular to it — this trick avoids needing to decompose the normal force and friction. Then mg decomposes into mg sin(theta) (along incline, down-slope) and mg cos(theta) (perpendicular to incline, into the surface).

Weight component parallel to incline = mg sin(theta) (newtons), pointing down-slope. Derived by projecting mg (always vertical) onto the tilted x-axis aligned with the incline surface. This is the force that accelerates the block down a frictionless ramp. AP FRQ checks (Science Practice 5: Calculations): when theta = 0 (flat), mg sin(0) = 0 — no horizontal pull. When theta = 90 (vertical wall), mg sin(90) = mg — full free fall. Always pair with the perpendicular component mg cos(theta) for N when summing forces in the perpendicular direction.

questions.prompt: Component of weight perpendicular to incline.
questions.explanation: Weight component perpendicular to incline = mg cos(theta) (newtons), pointing into the surface. Sets the normal force on a frictionless incline: N = mg cos(theta). When theta = 0 (flat surface), N = mg (full weight). When theta = 90 (vertical wall), N = 0 (block has no normal force). AP FRQs (Science Practice 5: Calculations) chain this with friction: f_max = mu_s mg cos(theta), so as theta increases mg sin(theta) eventually overcomes mu_s mg cos(theta), giving the critical angle tan(theta_c) = mu_s for slipping to begin.

On a frictionless incline with no other vertical applied force, N = mg cos(theta). This is the SAME value as the perpendicular weight component because perpendicular forces must sum to zero (no acceleration perpendicular to the incline — the block doesn't sink into or fly off the surface). AP FRQs (Science Practice 6: Argumentation) commonly catch students who write N = mg (forgetting the cos(theta) factor) — graders mark this conceptual error harshly. If a string parallel to the incline applies an additional force, N is unchanged; if the string pulls outward at an angle, N changes.

Acceleration of a block on a frictionless incline: a = g sin(theta) (m/s^2), directed down-slope. Derived by F_net = m a: mg sin(theta) = m a, so a = g sin(theta) — mass-independent (Galilean inertia result). On a rough incline a = g(sin(theta) - mu_k cos(theta)). AP FRQs (Science Practice 5: Calculations) use this to find time-to-bottom or final velocity using kinematics. Limiting cases: theta = 0 gives a = 0 (no slope, no motion); theta = 90 gives a = g (vertical free fall). Galileo verified this by rolling balls down inclined planes.

questions.prompt: Pulley with two masses connected by string.
choices: 2 m_1 m_2 g over (m_1 plus m_2)
questions.explanation: An Atwood machine, invented by George Atwood in 1784, consists of two masses connected by a string over a frictionless massless pulley. Designed to slow gravitational acceleration (a = (m_1 - m_2) g / (m_1 + m_2)) enough to time motion with 18th-century pendulum clocks. AP Physics 1 FRQs (Science Practice 1: Modeling) require separate free-body diagrams for each mass, then F = m a applied to each, with the constraint that both masses share the same |a| (rope is inextensible) and same |T| (rope is massless, pulley is massless and frictionless).

choices: 2 m_1 m_2 g over (m_1 plus m_2)
questions.explanation: In an Atwood machine, the heavier mass accelerates downward and the lighter mass upward. The system's center of mass falls because the unbalanced weight (m_1 - m_2) g is the net external force on the two-mass system. Limiting cases (Science Practice 6: Argumentation): if m_1 = m_2, a = 0 (system stays at rest or moves at constant velocity); if m_1 >> m_2, a → g (m_2 falls freely under gravity while pulling m_1 along). AP FRQs often ask students to predict and justify direction before computing magnitude — a Modeling step (Science Practice 1).

choices: (m1 minus m2) g over (m1 plus m2)
questions.explanation: Tension is equal on both sides of an ideal Atwood machine because the string is massless and the pulley is frictionless and massless (no torque needed to rotate it). Newton's second law applied to a massless rope: F_net = m a = 0 since m = 0, so net force is zero, meaning tensions at both ends balance — but only because mass is zero. AP FRQs (Science Practice 6) often test this with a follow-up: if the pulley has mass (rotational inertia), tensions become unequal because the pulley needs net torque to angularly accelerate — but that scenario is reserved for Unit 5.

choices: (m1 minus m2) g over (m1 plus m2)
questions.correctText: (m1 minus m2) g over (m1 plus m2)
questions.explanation: a = (m_1 - m_2) g / (m_1 + m_2) (m/s^2), where m_1 > m_2. Derived by writing F = m a for each mass: for m_1 (falling): m_1 g - T = m_1 a; for m_2 (rising): T - m_2 g = m_2 a. Adding eliminates T, giving the result. AP FRQs (Science Practice 5: Calculations) regularly test this derivation. Limiting checks: m_1 = m_2 gives a = 0; m_2 = 0 gives a = g (free fall of m_1); m_1 >> m_2 gives a ≈ g. This system is a classic AP Modeling FRQ.

choices: 2 m_1 m_2 g over (m_1 plus m_2)
questions.correctText: 2 m_1 m_2 g over (m_1 plus m_2)
questions.explanation: T = 2 m_1 m_2 g / (m_1 + m_2) (newtons). Derived by substituting a back into T - m_2 g = m_2 a, giving T = m_2(g + a) = 2 m_1 m_2 g / (m_1 + m_2). Limiting checks (Science Practice 6: Argumentation): if m_1 = m_2 = m, T = 2 m^2 g / (2m) = mg, the static equilibrium result; if m_2 → 0, T → 0 (lighter mass exerts no pull); if m_1 → infinity with m_2 fixed, T → 2 m_2 g — the rope tension approaches twice m_2's weight, the maximum non-breaking value. AP FRQs test the derivation explicitly.

questions.prompt: Force times distance in direction of force.
questions.explanation: Work W = F * d * cos(theta) (joules), the scalar product of force and displacement vectors. Coriolis and Poncelet formalized the modern definition in the 1820s-30s, building on Galilean and Newtonian foundations. Work is positive when force has a component along displacement, negative when opposing it, and zero when perpendicular (centripetal force does no work on circular motion). AP FRQs (Science Practice 5: Calculations) commonly require students to compute work done by individual forces (gravity, normal, friction, applied) and sum to get net work, then apply the work-energy theorem W_net = Delta KE.

W = F d cos(theta) (joules), where F is force magnitude (N), d is displacement magnitude (m), and theta is the angle between F and d. On the AP equation sheet. Maximum work when theta = 0 (force along motion); zero when theta = 90 (force perpendicular); negative when theta > 90 (force opposes motion). AP FRQs (Science Practice 1: Modeling) test work done by gravity on a projectile (W_g = -mg * h_rise during ascent), work done by friction on a slope (always negative, W_f = -f_k * d), and the result on a free-body force when its angle changes.

Joule (J) = 1 N * m = 1 kg * m^2 / s^2. Named after James Prescott Joule (1818-1889), whose paddle-wheel experiment established the mechanical equivalent of heat (4.184 J/cal) and validated conservation of energy. Joule's work proved heat and mechanical work are interchangeable forms of energy — foundational to thermodynamics. AP FRQs (Science Practice 2: Mathematical Routines) require unit-consistency checks: when a student computes W = (5 N)(2 m)(cos 0) = 10 J, the joule appears naturally. Other equivalent forms: kg * m^2/s^2 from KE, Pa * m^3 from pressure-volume work.

Work done by kinetic friction is ALWAYS negative: W_f = -f_k * d (joules), where d is path length traveled. Friction force opposes velocity, so cos(180) = -1. The negative sign reflects energy removed from the mechanical system and converted to thermal energy at the contact surfaces. AP FRQs (Science Practice 6: Argumentation) test this: a block slides on a rough surface and decelerates — students must explicitly invoke W_f < 0 to explain the kinetic-energy decrease, citing W_net = Delta KE. On a closed-loop path (round trip), W_f remains negative — friction is a non-conservative force.

Work in vertical motion: W = F_y * Delta y (joules), where F_y is the vertical force component and Delta y is the vertical displacement. For gravity: W_g = -m g Delta y when y is upward positive (gravity does negative work on rising objects, positive on falling). AP FRQs (Science Practice 5: Calculations) chain this with the work-energy theorem on roller-coaster and pendulum problems: W_g = -m g Delta y = Delta KE, recovering v_f^2 = v_0^2 - 2 g Delta y. Independent of horizontal motion — a key Galilean independence-of-motion result, applied to projectiles.

Kinetic energy KE = (1/2) m v^2 (joules) is the energy associated with motion. Always positive (or zero at rest), scalar. Leibniz (vis viva, 1686) and Coriolis (1829) developed the modern form. KE depends on the square of speed — doubling v quadruples KE — which is why high-speed crashes are so destructive and braking distance scales with v^2 (not v). AP FRQs (Science Practice 5: Calculations) chain KE with the work-energy theorem (W_net = Delta KE) and conservation of energy (KE + PE = constant) on roller coasters, pendulums, and inclined surfaces. On the AP equation sheet.

KE = (1/2) m v^2 (joules), with m in kg and v in m/s. The (1/2) factor arises from integrating F dx = m a dx with a = dv/dt and v = dx/dt, yielding KE = integral of m v dv = (1/2) m v^2. On the AP equation sheet. AP FRQs (Science Practice 2: Mathematical Routines) require KE comparisons: doubling v gives 4x KE; halving m at constant v halves KE. Symmetry check: KE is the same whether v is +5 m/s or -5 m/s — direction doesn't matter (KE is scalar). Translational KE only — rotational KE = (1/2) I omega^2 comes in Unit 5.

questions.prompt: Theorem connecting work to kinetic energy change.
questions.explanation: Work-Energy Theorem: W_net = Delta KE = KE_f - KE_i (joules). Net work done on an object equals its change in kinetic energy. Proven by integrating Newton's second law (F = m a) over displacement: W = integral F dx = integral m a dx = (1/2) m v_f^2 - (1/2) m v_0^2. On the AP equation sheet. AP FRQs (Science Practice 5: Calculations) use this to bypass kinematics: find final speed given total work without needing acceleration or time. Critical for problems with variable forces (springs) where F = m a is hard to apply directly because a changes.

questions.prompt: Doubling velocity multiplies KE by this.
questions.explanation: Doubling v multiplies KE by 4 because KE scales with v^2. This non-linear dependence has real-world consequences: stopping distance d = KE / (f_k) = (1/2) m v^2 / (mu_k m g) scales with v^2, so a car going 60 mph needs 4x the stopping distance of one going 30 mph. AP FRQs (Science Practice 6: Argumentation) test this in safety-engineering contexts and in collision-energy comparisons. Limiting case (Science Practice 2): tripling v multiplies KE by 9; halving v gives KE / 4. Often appears in paragraph-length arguments about car crashes.

KE is always non-negative because KE = (1/2) m v^2 with m > 0 and v^2 >= 0. Zero only when the object is at rest. This contrasts with potential energy, which can be negative (depending on chosen zero point) or with work, which can be negative (force opposes displacement). AP FRQs (Science Practice 6: Argumentation) test this conceptual distinction: students must recognize that even a falling object reversing direction has positive KE throughout (only v = 0 at the peak gives KE = 0 momentarily). Scalar quantity — direction-independent.

Potential energy U (joules) is stored energy associated with the configuration of a conservative-force system (position of an object in a force field). For gravity near Earth: U_g = m g h. For springs: U_s = (1/2) k x^2. Term coined by Rankine (1853). PE is a scalar but its sign depends on the chosen reference (zero) point. AP FRQs (Science Practice 1: Modeling) demand that students declare and label the reference level for U_g (the ground? table top? bottom of incline?) before applying conservation of mechanical energy KE + U = constant on roller coasters, pendulums, and spring-launched projectiles.

Gravitational PE U_g = m g h (joules) near Earth's surface, where h is height above a chosen reference. Linear in h because g is approximately constant near Earth's surface (within ~1% to altitude 30 km). Derived as -W_g = -(-m g h) = m g h for upward displacement. On the AP equation sheet. AP FRQs (Science Practice 5: Calculations) use this in conservation of energy: (1/2) m v_0^2 + m g h_0 = (1/2) m v_f^2 + m g h_f. Limiting case: at h = 0 reference level, U_g = 0. Mass cancels in pure free-fall problems — Galileo's equivalence principle.

Spring PE U_s = (1/2) k x^2 (joules), with k the spring constant (N/m) and x the displacement from equilibrium (m). Hooke (1660s) discovered F = -k x; PE follows from integrating F dx. On the AP equation sheet. Always non-negative because of x^2. AP FRQs (Science Practice 5: Calculations) use this in conservation of energy for mass-spring systems: (1/2) k x_0^2 = (1/2) m v_max^2 gives v_max = x_0 sqrt(k/m), the maximum speed at equilibrium. Quadratic in x means the energy stored grows fast with compression — a spring compressed by 2x stores 4x the energy.

The PE reference (zero) is arbitrary — only PE differences are physically meaningful, because forces depend on -dU/dx, the derivative. AP FRQs (Science Practice 1: Modeling) require students to explicitly state their chosen reference (e.g., 'taking U_g = 0 at the bottom of the incline') before computing PE values, because the same setup gives different absolute U but identical Delta U. Common choices: bottom of incline, ground level, lowest point of pendulum swing. Conservation of energy is unaffected by the choice because both initial and final U shift by the same constant.

questions.prompt: Negative work changes PE this way.
questions.explanation: Work done BY a conservative force on an object equals the negative of the change in PE: W_cons = -Delta U. So if the force does negative work (e.g., gravity on an upward-moving object), Delta U > 0 — PE increases. AP FRQs (Science Practice 6: Argumentation) test the sign convention: lifting a book against gravity, W_g = -mgh < 0, so U_g increases by mgh. Students writing paragraph-length arguments must explicitly invoke W_cons = -Delta U to justify mgh storage. Non-conservative forces (friction) have no associated PE — energy is dissipated as heat.

Total energy (KE + PE + thermal + chemical + ...) of a closed isolated system is conserved — the First Law of Thermodynamics. Established empirically by James Joule (1840s) with paddle-wheel experiments; formalized by Helmholtz (1847). AP Physics 1 typically tests mechanical energy conservation: E_mech = KE + U = constant when only conservative forces (gravity, springs) act. If friction is present, mechanical energy decreases (-W_f goes to thermal). AP FRQs (Science Practice 5: Calculations) use this to bridge initial and final states on roller coasters and pendulums.

Mechanical energy E_mech = KE + U (joules) — sum of kinetic and potential energies. Conserved when only conservative forces (gravity, springs) act. AP FRQs (Science Practice 5: Calculations) write conservation as: (1/2) m v_0^2 + m g h_0 + (1/2) k x_0^2 = (1/2) m v_f^2 + m g h_f + (1/2) k x_f^2. When friction acts, mechanical energy is NOT conserved: E_{mech,f} = E_{mech,i} - |W_f|, with the lost mechanical energy going to thermal. Paragraph-length arguments (Science Practice 7) must declare 'conservative forces only' before invoking conservation.

questions.prompt: Force doing no net work over closed loop.
questions.explanation: A conservative force does zero net work over any closed path — equivalently, its work between two points is path-independent. Examples: gravity, ideal spring forces, electrostatic forces. Each conservative force has an associated PE function U(r) with F = -dU/dr. Non-conservative forces (friction, air drag, applied human forces): path-dependent work, no PE. AP FRQs (Science Practice 6: Argumentation) require students to identify which forces in a scenario are conservative and which are not — only conservative forces contribute to PE, while non-conservative forces drain mechanical energy.

questions.prompt: Force whose work depends on path.
questions.explanation: A non-conservative force is one whose work depends on the path taken — friction is the canonical example. A longer path through friction loses more energy. No potential energy function exists. AP FRQs (Science Practice 6: Argumentation) test this with closed-loop scenarios: a block sliding around and returning to its starting point has the same KE only if no friction acted (W_net = 0 over closed path); if friction acted, KE has decreased by f_k * (total path length). Modified conservation: E_{mech,f} = E_{mech,i} + W_{nc}. Includes applied forces, friction, drag.

questions.prompt: Energy lost to friction becomes this.
questions.explanation: Mechanical energy lost to friction is converted to thermal (internal) energy of the contact surfaces — observable as a temperature increase. Joule's paddle-wheel experiments (1840s) proved this mechanical-equivalent-of-heat principle, with 1 cal = 4.184 J. AP FRQs (Science Practice 5: Calculations) ask students to compute thermal energy generated: E_th = |W_f| = f_k * d_path. In paragraph-length arguments (Science Practice 7) about car braking, students must explicitly identify the destination of the lost KE — brake pads heating up, eventually radiating to surroundings. Total energy is conserved when thermal is included.

Power P = dW/dt or dE/dt (watts) — the rate of energy transfer or work done. Scalar quantity. The watt was named after James Watt (1736-1819), whose steam-engine improvements drove the Industrial Revolution. AP FRQs (Science Practice 5: Calculations) require students to compute average power as W/t over a duration or instantaneous power as F * v at a moment. Power matters in engineering contexts: a car engine's horsepower determines top speed, since at max speed F_drive = F_drag and so P_engine = F_drag * v_max. Higher P enables higher v_max.

Average power P = W / t (watts), where W is work done in joules and t is time interval in seconds. Equivalently P = Delta E / t for any energy transfer. On the AP equation sheet. AP FRQs (Science Practice 5: Calculations) chain this with W = F d cos(theta): a 50 kg student climbing a 3 m staircase in 4 s does W = (50)(9.8)(3) = 1470 J of work against gravity, requiring P = 1470/4 ≈ 368 W (about a half-horsepower). Faster climbing → higher P at the same W. Units: 1 W = 1 J/s = 1 kg * m^2 / s^3.

Watt (W) = 1 J/s = 1 kg * m^2 / s^3. Named after James Watt (1736-1819), whose Newcomen-improved steam engine and condenser invention enabled the Industrial Revolution. AP FRQs (Science Practice 2: Mathematical Routines) require unit consistency: power computed from F * v (N * m/s) automatically gives W. Engineering equivalents: 1 horsepower (hp) = 745.7 W (a horse pulling 550 lb at 1 ft/s); 1 kilowatt-hour (energy) = 3.6 * 10^6 J. Common AP FRQ trap: students mistakenly use W as the symbol for both watt (unit) and work (variable) — context disambiguates.

questions.prompt: Power formula using force and velocity.
questions.explanation: Instantaneous power P = F * v * cos(theta) (watts) — the dot product of force and velocity vectors. Derived from P = dW/dt = F * (dx/dt) = F v. On the AP equation sheet. AP FRQs (Science Practice 5: Calculations) use this for cars and engines: at top speed v_max (where a = 0), F_drive = F_drag, so P = F_drag * v_max determines the maximum cruising speed achievable with a given engine. Doubling drag force halves v_max at constant P. For a constant-force application (motor lifting a load at steady v): P = F * v = m g v (overcoming gravity).

questions.prompt: Engineering unit of power equal to 745.7 watts.
questions.explanation: Horsepower (hp) = 745.7 W. James Watt defined 1 hp in 1782 as the rate of work a draft horse could sustain — lifting 550 lb a distance of 1 ft in 1 s — to market his steam engines in comparison units. AP FRQs (Science Practice 2: Mathematical Routines) occasionally include hp-to-watt conversion. A modern compact car engine: ~150 hp ≈ 112 kW; an industrial-scale gas turbine: 100,000+ hp. Watt's contribution shifted Western economies from animal/muscle/water power to thermodynamic-engine power in a single generation — a turning point in industrial history.

Momentum p = m v (kg * m/s), a vector with the same direction as velocity. Conserved in isolated systems (no net external force), per Newton's third-law reasoning. Descartes proposed an early scalar version (1644); Huygens corrected it to a vector (1669) by analyzing elastic collisions. AP FRQs (Science Practice 5: Calculations) chain conservation of momentum across collisions: p_i,1 + p_i,2 = p_f,1 + p_f,2. Vector nature is critical: in 2D collisions, x and y components must conserve independently, and a student writing 'momentum is conserved' on a paragraph-length argument must justify F_ext = 0.

Momentum unit: kg * m/s = N * s (the impulse-momentum equivalence). Also equal to J * s / m, though that form rarely appears. AP FRQs (Science Practice 2: Mathematical Routines) require unit consistency in conservation equations: m v on both sides must match. A 1500 kg car at 25 m/s has p = 37,500 kg * m/s — a useful gut-check magnitude. Note the equivalence with N * s: a 50 N force applied for 0.5 s delivers impulse J = 25 N * s = 25 kg * m/s of momentum change. Vector quantity — sign convention from the chosen +x axis must be tracked.

Momentum is a vector p = m v, inheriting direction from velocity. This makes 1D collision problems require signed quantities: an east-moving p is positive, west-moving negative. In 2D collisions (a classic AP FRQ scenario like billiards), momentum is conserved separately in x and y: p_{x,i,total} = p_{x,f,total} and p_{y,i,total} = p_{y,f,total}. AP FRQ traps (Science Practice 6: Argumentation): students must distinguish between |Delta p| (magnitude of vector difference) and Delta |p| (change in scalar magnitude). For circular motion at constant speed, |p| is constant but Delta p is nonzero (direction changes).

questions.prompt: Total momentum of system conserved when.
questions.explanation: Total momentum of a system is conserved when the net external force on the system is zero (F_ext = 0). Internal forces (collision forces between system members) cancel by Newton's third law. AP FRQ paragraph-length arguments (Science Practice 7) must explicitly invoke 'isolated system' or 'F_ext = 0' before applying p_i = p_f. Common scenarios: explosion (no external horizontal force, so horizontal p is conserved); collision (during the brief collision, external forces are negligible compared to collision forces); rocket propulsion (consider rocket + fuel as one system).

questions.prompt: Newton's second law in momentum form.
questions.explanation: F_net = dp/dt — Newton's original statement of his second law (Principia, 1687), more general than F = m a. For constant mass dp/dt = m(dv/dt) = m a, recovering F = m a. For variable mass (rockets expelling fuel) or relativistic cases, the momentum form is essential. AP Physics 1 FRQs (Science Practice 5: Calculations) use the integral form: integral F dt = Delta p (impulse). This bridges dynamics and momentum analysis on collision problems. Limiting case: F = 0 gives dp/dt = 0, recovering momentum conservation.

Impulse J = F * Delta t (newton-seconds), the integral of force over time interval. Vector. Equals the change in momentum: J = Delta p (impulse-momentum theorem). On the AP equation sheet. AP FRQs (Science Practice 5: Calculations) compute impulse from constant force * time, or from the area under a force-vs-time graph for variable force. Practical applications: airbags increase Delta t to reduce average F for the same Delta p (whiplash injury reduction); follow-through in sports increases contact time and so total Delta p; pole-vault landing mats absorb impact by stretching Delta t.

questions.prompt: Theorem connecting impulse to momentum change.
questions.explanation: Impulse-Momentum Theorem: J = Delta p, where J = F_avg * Delta t (N * s) and Delta p = m * Delta v (kg * m/s). Derived by integrating F = dp/dt over time. On the AP equation sheet. AP FRQs (Science Practice 5: Calculations) use this for collision problems where time of contact is given: a 0.15 kg baseball decelerates from 40 m/s to 0 in 0.001 s during catch, requiring F_avg = m Delta v / Delta t = (0.15)(40)/0.001 = 6000 N. Critical for safety design (airbags, helmet padding, crumple zones) — extending Delta t reduces peak F for the same Delta p.

Impulse unit: N * s = kg * m/s (same as momentum, by the impulse-momentum theorem J = Delta p). AP FRQs (Science Practice 2: Mathematical Routines) often blend the two: 'A force of 20 N applied for 0.5 s on a 2 kg cart initially at rest' → J = (20)(0.5) = 10 N*s → Delta p = 10 kg*m/s → Delta v = 5 m/s. Students must show unit cancellation explicitly to receive full credit on calculation FRQs. The equivalence reinforces the conceptual unity of impulse (cause: force over time) and momentum change (effect).

Impulse equals the area under a force-vs-time graph: J = integral F dt = Delta p. For non-constant forces (collision force-time profile typically rises and falls), area under the curve gives total impulse. AP FRQs (Science Practice 4: Data Analysis) hand students a force-time graph from a sensor on a colliding cart, asking them to estimate J using triangle/trapezoid approximation, then compute Delta v = J/m. The graph approach handles variable forces that F = m a cannot — a powerful tool. Average force F_avg = J / Delta t (the rectangle of equivalent area).

Extending the contact time Delta t reduces the average force F_avg required to deliver a given impulse: F_avg = J / Delta t = Delta p / Delta t. This is the engineering principle behind airbags, crumple zones, helmets, bungee cords, parkour rolls, and bend-the-knees landings. AP FRQs (Science Practice 6: Argumentation) test this in paragraph-length arguments about safety design: students must explicitly state that for fixed Delta p (the change in momentum during impact), increasing Delta t decreases the maximum force experienced by the body, reducing injury. Galileo and Newton implicit; explicit engineering use is 20th-century.

An elastic collision conserves BOTH momentum AND kinetic energy: p_i = p_f and KE_i = KE_f. Idealizations: rigid steel balls, billiard balls (nearly elastic), atomic collisions. AP FRQs (Science Practice 5: Calculations) use the joint conservation as two equations in two unknowns (two final velocities) — solvable algebraically. Useful 1D result: for equal masses, the velocities swap (Newton's cradle). For m_1 hitting stationary m_2: v_{1,f} = (m_1 - m_2)/(m_1 + m_2) * v_{1,i}, v_{2,f} = 2 m_1 /(m_1+m_2) * v_{1,i}. Limiting case: m_1 >> m_2 gives v_{2,f} ≈ 2 v_{1,i}.

An inelastic collision conserves momentum but NOT kinetic energy — some KE converts to thermal, deformation, sound, or internal energy. AP FRQs (Science Practice 5: Calculations) test this with cars deforming on impact, balls of clay merging, or asteroid impacts. Students use p_i = p_f to find final velocities, then compute Delta KE = KE_f - KE_i to find energy lost. Always Delta KE < 0 in inelastic collisions (energy goes to deformation). On paragraph-length arguments (Science Practice 7), students must explicitly identify the destinations of the lost KE (heat, sound, deformation work).

A perfectly inelastic collision is the most extreme inelastic case: objects stick together post-collision and move as a single body. Maximum KE loss possible while still conserving momentum. AP FRQ (Science Practice 5: Calculations): m_1 v_1 + m_2 v_2 = (m_1 + m_2) v_f, giving v_f = (m_1 v_1 + m_2 v_2)/(m_1 + m_2). Fraction of KE lost: (KE_i - KE_f)/KE_i = m_2/(m_1 + m_2) for m_2 initially at rest. Classic example: bullet embedding in a block (ballistic pendulum, Robins 1742). Conservation of energy fails; conservation of momentum holds because collision forces are internal.

Momentum is ALWAYS conserved in collisions of an isolated system, regardless of elastic vs inelastic. The reason (Science Practice 6: Argumentation): during the brief collision, the very large internal forces between colliding bodies (Newton's third law pair) cancel in the total system's momentum equation. External forces (gravity, friction) act for so brief a time that their impulse is negligible. AP FRQs require students to explicitly state 'no significant external impulse during collision' as justification before applying p_i,total = p_f,total to bridge before-and-after states.

Kinetic energy is conserved only in elastic collisions; lost (converted to heat, sound, deformation) in inelastic collisions. AP FRQs (Science Practice 5: Calculations) ask students to test whether a collision was elastic by computing KE_i and KE_f from given velocities — if equal, elastic; if KE_f < KE_i, inelastic. Limiting case: in a perfectly inelastic collision with m_2 initially at rest, exactly m_2/(m_1 + m_2) of the original KE is converted (the rest goes into translational KE of the combined mass). Energy is never destroyed — only converted to non-mechanical forms.

choices: Sum m_i x_i over sum m_i
questions.explanation: Center of mass (COM): the mass-weighted average position of a system. For discrete masses: x_COM = sum(m_i x_i) / sum(m_i). For continuous distributions: integral. Newton recognized that an extended body behaves like a point mass at its COM when external forces are applied. AP FRQs (Science Practice 1: Modeling) treat collisions and explosions by tracking the system's COM, which moves with constant velocity if F_ext = 0 (Newton's first law applies to the COM). Useful intuition: the COM of two equal masses is at their midpoint; for unequal masses, COM is closer to the larger one.

choices: Sum m_i x_i over sum m_i
questions.correctText: Sum m_i x_i over sum m_i
questions.explanation: x_COM = (m_1 x_1 + m_2 x_2 + ...) / (m_1 + m_2 + ...) — mass-weighted average of position. Same form for y_COM and z_COM in 2D/3D. AP FRQs (Science Practice 5: Calculations) compute COM of irregular composite objects by treating each piece as a point at its individual COM, then weighting. For symmetric uniform objects, COM is at the geometric center. For an L-shape or partial-cutout, COM moves toward the more massive section. Limiting case: equal masses give the midpoint. Used to track explosion-fragment motion: COM continues unchanged.

Motion of the center of mass: F_ext,net = M_total * a_COM (Newton's second law for the system as a whole). Internal forces (between system members) cannot accelerate the COM. AP FRQ paragraph-length arguments (Science Practice 7) leverage this: in an isolated explosion (F_ext = 0), COM continues at the pre-explosion velocity — fragments scatter, but the COM trajectory is unchanged. In an isolated collision, COM velocity stays the same. In free fall, COM has a = g regardless of internal rotations or fragmentations. This insight simplifies many AP problems dramatically.

questions.prompt: Center of mass of uniform rod.
choices: Sum m_i x_i over sum m_i
questions.explanation: Center of mass of a uniform rod: the geometric midpoint, by symmetry. For a non-uniform rod (variable linear density), COM shifts toward the denser end and requires integration. AP FRQs (Science Practice 5: Calculations) often test COM of composite systems with a uniform rod plus point masses at the ends — students use the weighted-average formula. For symmetric uniform shapes: rectangle COM at intersection of diagonals; disk COM at geometric center; sphere COM at sphere center. Pivot a rod at COM and it balances horizontally — the experimental test for COM location.

In an explosion with no external force, the COM of the system continues at the pre-explosion velocity. Internal explosion forces (gunpowder, chemical pressure) are internal, so by Newton's third law they cancel in the total-system equation. AP FRQ (Science Practice 5: Calculations): a 10 kg projectile at v = 20 m/s explodes at peak into two equal 5 kg fragments. If fragment 1 has v_1 = 30 m/s in the original direction, fragment 2's velocity satisfies 10*20 = 5*30 + 5*v_2, giving v_2 = 10 m/s. The COM trajectory remains a parabola under gravity, unaffected by the explosion.

questions.prompt: Quantity always conserved in isolated system.
questions.explanation: Momentum is conserved in any isolated system (F_ext = 0). The vector sum p_total = m_1 v_1 + m_2 v_2 + ... is constant in time. Newton stated this implicitly via F = dp/dt; Descartes and Huygens established it empirically for collisions in the 1600s. AP FRQ paragraph-length arguments (Science Practice 7) require students to explicitly justify 'F_ext = 0' or 'isolated system' before invoking conservation. Common AP scenarios: 1D collision on frictionless track (horizontal F_ext = 0 even if gravity acts vertically); 2D collision (each component conserves separately); explosion in free space.

An isolated system has no external forces acting on it (or, weaker definition, the net external impulse is negligible). Internal forces (between system members) cannot change total momentum because Newton's third law makes them cancel pairwise. AP FRQs (Science Practice 1: Modeling) require students to define their system explicitly — boundaries matter. For a colliding pair on a horizontal frictionless surface, the system (m_1, m_2) is isolated horizontally (gravity and normal are vertical, balanced) so horizontal momentum is conserved. Adding the surface to the system makes it isolated in 3D.

choices: m_1 v_1 plus m_2 v_2 equals constant
questions.correctText: m_1 v_1 plus m_2 v_2 equals constant
questions.explanation: Conservation of momentum for two-body collision: m_1 v_{1,i} + m_2 v_{2,i} = m_1 v_{1,f} + m_2 v_{2,f} (kg * m/s). Each term is a vector — for 2D collisions, x and y components conserve independently. AP FRQs (Science Practice 5: Calculations) chain this with KE conservation for elastic collisions (two equations, two unknowns) or with the constraint v_{1,f} = v_{2,f} for perfectly inelastic ones. Sign convention: pick a +x axis; opposite-direction velocities take negative signs. Common AP scenario: head-on 1D collision of carts on an air track.

In an elastic collision, total KE is conserved: (1/2) m_1 v_{1,i}^2 + (1/2) m_2 v_{2,i}^2 = (1/2) m_1 v_{1,f}^2 + (1/2) m_2 v_{2,f}^2. Combined with momentum conservation, gives two algebraic equations in two unknowns (the final velocities). AP FRQs (Science Practice 5: Calculations) use this to solve cleanly for v_{1,f} and v_{2,f}. Useful result for equal masses: velocities swap exactly (Newton's cradle). Real collisions are never perfectly elastic; AP idealizes steel balls, billiard balls, atomic collisions as elastic for problem-solving.

In an inelastic collision, KE is NOT conserved — but TOTAL energy is conserved when accounting for thermal, sound, and deformation energy: KE_i = KE_f + E_thermal + E_deform + E_sound. AP FRQs (Science Practice 5: Calculations) compute Delta KE = KE_f - KE_i (always negative for inelastic). For perfectly inelastic with m_2 at rest: fraction of KE retained = m_1/(m_1 + m_2); fraction lost = m_2/(m_1 + m_2). Paragraph-length arguments (Science Practice 7) must identify where the lost KE went — students often write 'lost as heat and sound and deformation work.'

choices: 2 m_1 m_2 g over (m_1 plus m_2)
questions.explanation: Angular velocity omega = d(theta)/dt (rad/s), the rotational analog of linear velocity. Vector pointing along the axis of rotation (right-hand rule). AP Physics 1 typically treats omega as a signed scalar for fixed-axis rotation. Period T and frequency f relate by T = 2 pi / omega = 1/f. AP FRQs (Science Practice 5: Calculations) connect linear and angular: v = r omega for a point at radius r on a rigid rotating body. For a wheel rolling without slipping, v_COM = R omega — the rolling constraint. Newton's framework extended to rotations by Euler (1750s).

questions.prompt: Rate of change of angular velocity.
questions.explanation: Angular acceleration alpha = d(omega)/dt (rad/s^2), the rotational analog of linear acceleration. Caused by net torque (tau_net = I alpha). For constant alpha, rotational kinematic equations parallel linear ones: theta = theta_0 + omega_0 t + (1/2) alpha t^2; omega = omega_0 + alpha t; omega^2 = omega_0^2 + 2 alpha Delta theta. AP FRQs (Science Practice 5: Calculations) commonly invoke these in pulley problems (a rope around a pulley exerts torque on the pulley) and in rotating-platform problems. Linear analog: a = r alpha for a point at radius r.

Angular velocity unit: rad/s (radians per second). Radians are dimensionless (arc length / radius), so technically the unit is 1/s — but AP Physics 1 conventionally writes rad/s for clarity. 2 pi rad = 360 degrees = one full revolution. Conversion: omega (rad/s) = 2 pi f (Hz) = 2 pi / T (s). A turntable at 33 rpm: omega = 33 * (2 pi / 60) ≈ 3.5 rad/s. AP FRQs (Science Practice 2: Mathematical Routines) demand radians (not degrees) when using rotational equations because v = r omega and a = r alpha are derived assuming radian measure.

v = r omega (m/s), tangential linear velocity of a point at radius r on a rotating rigid body. On the AP equation sheet. Derived from arc length s = r theta, then differentiated: ds/dt = r d(theta)/dt = r omega. AP FRQs (Science Practice 5: Calculations) chain this with the rolling constraint: a wheel rolling without slipping has v_COM = R omega and a_COM = R alpha. Limiting case: at the wheel-ground contact point, v = v_COM - R omega = 0 (instantaneously at rest — that's why static friction acts, not kinetic). Similarly, a_tangential = r alpha for a point at radius r.

T = 1/f, where T is period (s) and f is frequency (Hz = 1/s). Also: T = 2 pi / omega and omega = 2 pi f. AP FRQs (Science Practice 2: Mathematical Routines) use these in oscillation and rotation problems: a pendulum with T = 1 s has f = 1 Hz and omega = 2 pi rad/s. Earth's rotation: T = 24 hours = 86,400 s, so omega ≈ 7.27 * 10^-5 rad/s (very slow). On a spinning bicycle wheel rotating at 5 rev/s: f = 5 Hz, omega = 10 pi rad/s, and the rim speed v = R omega for tire radius R.

Torque tau (N * m) is the rotational analog of force — what causes angular acceleration. tau = r F sin(theta), the cross product of position vector r (from pivot) and force vector F. Vector quantity (along rotation axis by right-hand rule). Archimedes (3rd century BCE) understood torque qualitatively via the lever principle ('Give me a lever long enough and I shall move the world'). AP FRQs (Science Practice 5: Calculations) compute tau_net on extended bodies and apply tau_net = I alpha to find angular acceleration. Equilibrium requires both F_net = 0 (translational) and tau_net = 0 (rotational).

tau = r F sin(theta) (N * m), where r is the distance from pivot to force application point (m), F is force magnitude (N), and theta is the angle between r and F. On the AP equation sheet. Equivalent forms: tau = F * (r sin(theta)) = F * d (force times perpendicular distance, where d is the 'moment arm') OR tau = (F sin(theta)) * r (perpendicular force component times r). AP FRQs (Science Practice 5: Calculations) often have a force at an angle — students must identify the moment arm or perpendicular component. Maximum torque at theta = 90 (perpendicular force).

Maximum torque occurs when force is perpendicular to position vector (theta = 90 degrees, sin(90) = 1). Intuition: pushing on a door perpendicular to the door is far more effective than pushing parallel to it (which has zero moment arm). AP FRQ scenarios (Science Practice 6: Argumentation): why does pulling on a wrench at the far end produce more torque? Because increasing r at fixed F and theta scales tau. Why pull perpendicular? Because sin(90) = 1 maximizes the formula. Students writing paragraph-length arguments must explicitly invoke tau = r F sin(theta) and identify which factor is being optimized.

Torque unit: N * m (newton-meter). Dimensionally same as energy (J = N * m), but conceptually distinct — torque is a vector, energy is a scalar; torque applies at a specific axis, energy does not. AP FRQs (Science Practice 2: Mathematical Routines) require students to label tau in N * m, not J, to maintain clarity. Common practical magnitudes: tightening a bolt on a car engine (~50 N*m); a torque wrench limiting bolt torque to manufacturer spec; a torque produced by a 50 kg person standing on a 2 m diving board (1000 N * 2 m = 2000 N*m at the support).

Direction of torque vector: right-hand rule. Curl fingers of right hand from r toward F; thumb points along tau. For a wheel rotated counterclockwise as viewed from +z, tau points in +z direction. AP Physics 1 typically treats fixed-axis rotation, where tau is signed (CCW = +, CW = -). AP FRQs (Science Practice 1: Modeling) require explicit sign convention. The vector form (tau = r x F) becomes essential in AP Physics C, but Physics 1 can rely on the scalar form with rotation-sense convention. Right-hand rule emerges naturally from Newtonian mechanics extended to rotation by Euler.

Moment of inertia I (kg * m^2) is the rotational analog of mass — the rotational inertia, resistance to angular acceleration. Newton's second law for rotation: tau_net = I alpha. Depends on mass distribution relative to the rotation axis: I = sum(m_i r_i^2) for discrete masses; I = integral r^2 dm for continuous distributions. Same body has DIFFERENT I depending on the axis chosen. AP FRQs (Science Practice 1: Modeling) require students to identify the rotation axis before selecting the formula for I (a thin rod about end vs. about center have I = (1/3) ML^2 vs. (1/12) ML^2).

questions.prompt: Moment of inertia of point mass.
questions.explanation: I = m r^2 (kg * m^2) for a point mass m at distance r from the axis of rotation. The simplest case — building block for all extended-body I calculations via summation or integration. AP FRQs (Science Practice 5: Calculations) chain this with parallel-axis theorem (I = I_COM + m d^2) or use it directly for ball-and-string systems (ball treated as a point at distance r from pivot). Quadratic in r: doubling r quadruples I, making the system 4x harder to angularly accelerate. Important: I depends on mass distribution, not just mass total.

questions.prompt: Moment of inertia of solid sphere.
questions.explanation: I = (2/5) m r^2 for a uniform solid sphere rotating about a central axis through its center. Hollow shell sphere: I = (2/3) m r^2 (different mass distribution — mass at larger r on average). AP FRQs (Science Practice 5: Calculations) use solid-sphere I for rolling problems: a solid ball rolling down an incline has a = (5/7) g sin(theta), slower than a frictionless block (a = g sin(theta)). Comparison rolling race (Science Practice 6: Argumentation): solid sphere (2/5) beats hollow sphere (2/3) beats solid cylinder (1/2) beats hollow cylinder (1) — lower I/mr^2 ratio wins.

questions.prompt: Moment of inertia of thin rod about end.
questions.explanation: I = (1/3) m L^2 for a thin rod of length L rotating about one end (perpendicular to rod). About its center, I = (1/12) m L^2. AP FRQs (Science Practice 5: Calculations) classic: a falling rod, hinged at one end, has gravity acting at COM (L/2 from hinge); torque tau = m g (L/2) and I = (1/3) m L^2, giving alpha = (3/2) g/L. Parallel-axis theorem connects these: I_end = I_COM + m (L/2)^2 = (1/12) m L^2 + (1/4) m L^2 = (1/3) m L^2. Rotation about an end is 4x harder than about center.

I = (1/2) m R^2 for a uniform solid disk (or solid cylinder) rotating about its central axis. For a thin hollow ring: I = m R^2 (all mass at radius R — maximum I for given mass and outer radius). AP FRQs (Science Practice 5: Calculations) use disk I for pulleys with mass: torque from a hanging weight gives angular acceleration alpha = tau / I, and the tension on one side differs from the other because of pulley's rotational inertia (T_1 - T_2 = I alpha / R). Rolling-down-incline competition: disk (1/2) beats ring (1) — less I means faster acceleration.

questions.prompt: Newton's second law in rotational form.
questions.explanation: tau_net = I alpha — the rotational form of Newton's second law (Euler's reformulation of Newton, mid-18th century). Net external torque equals moment of inertia times angular acceleration. On the AP equation sheet. AP FRQs (Science Practice 5: Calculations) apply this after computing tau_net from individual torques (each tau = r F sin(theta)) and I from rotation axis. Limiting cases: tau_net = 0 means alpha = 0 (rotational equilibrium or constant omega); I = 0 (impossible for real bodies) would give infinite alpha. Always pair with linear Newton's second for problems with combined translation + rotation.

Moment of inertia I (kg * m^2) plays the role of mass in rotational dynamics: tau = I alpha mirrors F = m a. Greater I means greater resistance to angular acceleration for the same applied torque. Unlike mass (a single scalar for a body), I depends on the axis of rotation — a thin rod has I = (1/12) ML^2 about its center but (1/3) ML^2 about an end. AP FRQs (Science Practice 1: Modeling) require students to identify both the axis and the mass distribution before computing I. Used in tau_net = I alpha for linked angular kinematics.

Torque tau (N * m) is the rotational analog of force — what causes angular acceleration: tau = I alpha, analogous to F = m a. tau = r F sin(theta) for a force F applied at radius r at angle theta to r. AP FRQs (Science Practice 1: Modeling) require explicit identification of every torque acting on an extended body (each with sign — CCW positive convention). Net torque drives angular acceleration. Internal torques between particles cancel by Newton's third law extended to rotation; only external torques matter for tau_net.

Angular acceleration alpha (rad/s^2) is the rotational analog of linear acceleration a. Connected to tangential linear acceleration of a point at radius r: a_t = r alpha. Driven by net torque: alpha = tau_net / I. AP FRQs (Science Practice 5: Calculations) frequently link translational and rotational accelerations using the rolling-without-slipping constraint (a_COM = R alpha) for objects rolling down inclines. Constant-alpha kinematic equations parallel constant-a kinematics — students apply the rotational equivalents in pulley spin-up and turntable problems.

tau_net = 0 means alpha = 0 (no angular acceleration) — rotational equilibrium. The object either spins at constant omega or stays at rest. Analogous to F_net = 0 giving a = 0 (translational equilibrium). AP FRQs (Science Practice 5: Calculations) require BOTH F_net = 0 AND tau_net = 0 for full static equilibrium. Classic AP problem: a beam supported by two ropes, sum of vertical forces zero, sum of horizontal forces zero, AND sum of torques about ANY pivot zero. Choosing a clever pivot (where an unknown force acts) eliminates that variable from the torque equation — a powerful problem-solving trick.

Rotational KE: KE_rot = (1/2) I omega^2 (joules), the rotational analog of (1/2) m v^2. On the AP equation sheet. Vector neutrality: always non-negative. For a rotating disk with I = (1/2) m R^2 and omega = 10 rad/s of mass m = 2 kg, R = 0.5 m: KE_rot = (1/2)(0.25)(100) = 12.5 J. AP FRQs (Science Practice 5: Calculations) compute KE_rot for spinning flywheels, rotating dumbbells, and spinning skaters. Conserved when no net external torque does work over time (analogous to no net force doing work).

Total KE of a rolling object: KE = (1/2) m v_COM^2 + (1/2) I omega^2 (joules). For rolling without slipping: omega = v_COM / R, so KE = (1/2)(m + I/R^2) v_COM^2. AP FRQs (Science Practice 5: Calculations) use this in race-down-incline problems: a hollow ball (I = (2/3) m R^2) has more KE_rot per v_COM^2 than a solid ball, so its v_COM at the bottom is lower — solid balls win. Conservation of energy: m g h = (1/2)(m + I/R^2) v_COM^2 gives v_COM^2 = 2 g h / (1 + I/(m R^2)).

Rotational power P = tau * omega (watts), the rotational analog of P = F * v. AP FRQs (Science Practice 5: Calculations) use this for motors and engines: an electric motor providing tau = 5 N*m at omega = 100 rad/s delivers P = 500 W. Linear/rotational analogies: F ↔ tau, v ↔ omega, m ↔ I, a ↔ alpha, KE = (1/2)mv^2 ↔ (1/2)I omega^2, W = Fd ↔ W = tau * theta. For a torque ramping a flywheel from rest to omega_f, average P = tau * omega_avg = tau * omega_f / 2 (constant tau case).

Work done by torque: W_rot = tau * theta (joules) for constant torque over angular displacement theta (radians). For variable torque: W = integral tau d(theta), the area under tau-vs-theta graph. AP FRQs (Science Practice 5: Calculations) use this in conservation-of-energy problems with rotating bodies: net work done by net torque equals change in rotational KE (Work-Energy Theorem for rotation). Linear analog: W = F * d. Combined translation+rotation problems use both forms simultaneously, or use total KE = (1/2)(m + I/R^2) v_COM^2 for rolling without slipping.

On an incline, an object rolling without slipping accelerates faster if it has SMALLER I (per mass and radius squared). Reason (Science Practice 6: Argumentation): less energy is locked into rotation, more available for translation. Race ranking: solid sphere (I = 2/5 m R^2) > solid cylinder (1/2 m R^2) > hollow sphere (2/3 m R^2) > hollow cylinder/ring (m R^2). Acceleration: a = g sin(theta) / (1 + I/(mR^2)). Frictionless block (no rotation, all translation) wins everything: a = g sin(theta). AP FRQ classic: predict, justify, then verify experimentally.

Angular momentum L (kg * m^2 / s) is the rotational analog of linear momentum p. For a rotating rigid body: L = I omega. For a point mass: L = m v r sin(theta) = r * p_perp. Vector along rotation axis (right-hand rule). Newton's-third-law analog (Euler): internal torques cancel pairwise, so total L is conserved if no external torque acts. AP FRQs (Science Practice 5: Calculations) use conservation of L to explain skater spin-up (pulling arms in reduces I, so omega increases to keep L constant) and orbital mechanics (planets sweep equal areas in equal times — Kepler's second law).

Angular momentum of a rigid body about a fixed axis: L = I omega (kg * m^2 / s), where I is moment of inertia (kg * m^2) and omega is angular velocity (rad/s). On the AP equation sheet. For a point mass at distance r from axis with linear velocity v perpendicular to r: L = m v r. AP FRQs (Science Practice 5: Calculations) chain conservation: L_i = L_f, so I_i omega_i = I_f omega_f. Skater pulls arms in: I decreases ~4x, so omega increases ~4x (because L = I omega is conserved). KE_rot = (1/2) I omega^2 INCREASES, however — the skater does work pulling arms inward.

choices: v equals v_0 plus a t
questions.explanation: Angular momentum unit: kg * m^2 / s. Also writeable as N * m * s (the torque-time analog of impulse-momentum). AP FRQs (Science Practice 2: Mathematical Routines) require unit consistency: I (kg*m^2) * omega (rad/s = 1/s) gives kg*m^2/s automatically. Earth's spin angular momentum: ~7 * 10^33 kg*m^2/s. A spinning bicycle wheel: ~0.5 kg*m^2/s. Quantum analog (h-bar = 1.05 * 10^-34 J*s) appears in AP Physics 2 atomic structure — angular momentum is quantized at atomic scale, with electron orbital L in multiples of h-bar.

questions.prompt: Conservation of angular momentum requires this.
questions.explanation: Angular momentum is conserved when net external torque on the system is zero (tau_ext = 0). Internal torques cancel pairwise (Newton's third law for rotation). AP FRQ paragraph-length arguments (Science Practice 7) require students to explicitly justify 'no external torque' before invoking L_i = L_f. Common scenarios: figure skater (gravity and normal force balance — no horizontal external torque); spinning satellite in space (no torque from gravity if symmetric); a person on a turntable changing pose (no external torque about the central axis). Note: external forces CAN act without producing torque (if force passes through axis).

questions.prompt: Skater pulling arms in changes this.
questions.explanation: When a figure skater pulls arms inward, I decreases (mass closer to spin axis), so omega INCREASES to conserve L = I omega. If I drops by factor 4, omega rises by factor 4. AP FRQ (Science Practice 5: Calculations) classic: I_i = 5 kg*m^2 at omega_i = 2 rad/s gives L = 10 kg*m^2/s; if skater pulls in to I_f = 2 kg*m^2, then omega_f = L/I_f = 5 rad/s. KE_rot INCREASES from (1/2)(5)(4) = 10 J to (1/2)(2)(25) = 25 J — the skater does +15 J of work pulling against centripetal forces of their arms (Science Practice 6: Argumentation).

questions.prompt: Quantity conserved with no external torque.
questions.explanation: Angular momentum L is conserved when net external torque on the system is zero (tau_ext = 0). The rotational analog of momentum conservation under zero net external force. AP FRQs (Science Practice 5: Calculations) use this for skater spin-up, pulsar formation (collapsing star spins up because I decreases drastically), and orbital mechanics. Kepler's second law (1609) — planets sweep equal areas in equal times — is angular momentum conservation in action: L = m v r sin(theta) is constant, so when r decreases (perihelion), v increases proportionally. Newton later showed Kepler's laws follow from gravity + L conservation.

choices: m_1 v_1 plus m_2 v_2 equals constant
questions.explanation: Conservation of linear momentum: when F_ext = 0 on an isolated system, total momentum p = sum(m_i v_i) is constant. The cornerstone of collision analysis. AP FRQs (Science Practice 5: Calculations) apply this universally to collisions (elastic, inelastic, perfectly inelastic), explosions, and any system where the timescale of interaction is brief enough that external impulses (gravity, friction) are negligible. Paragraph-length arguments (Science Practice 7) about colliding carts on a frictionless track: external horizontal forces are zero, so horizontal momentum is conserved through the collision.

Conservation of angular momentum: when tau_ext = 0 on an isolated rotational system, total L = sum(I_i omega_i) is constant. The rotational analog of momentum conservation. AP FRQs (Science Practice 5: Calculations) apply this to: (1) figure skater pulling arms in (I decreases, omega increases); (2) collapsing stars forming pulsars (I drops by factor 10^10, omega rises proportionally — neutron stars rotate millisecond periods); (3) orbital mechanics (Kepler's second law); (4) collisions between rotating and translating systems (a rod struck off-center conserves angular momentum about a chosen axis).

questions.prompt: Conservation law for isolated system energy.
choices: 2 m_1 m_2 g over (m_1 plus m_2)
questions.explanation: Conservation of energy (first law of thermodynamics): total energy of an isolated system is constant — only converts between forms. Joule established the mechanical equivalent of heat (1840s), Helmholtz formalized the conservation law (1847). AP FRQs (Science Practice 5: Calculations) use mechanical energy conservation (KE + U = constant) when only conservative forces act, and total energy conservation (including thermal) when friction is present. Paragraph-length arguments (Science Practice 7) must declare which form of energy each step transforms to, especially in inelastic collisions where KE → thermal + sound + deformation.

questions.prompt: Skater spinning faster by pulling arms.
questions.explanation: A skater pulling arms in spins faster — angular momentum conservation in action. Mechanism (Science Practice 6: Argumentation): the skater applies internal forces to bring mass closer to the spin axis, reducing I. Because no external torque acts (gravity and normal force are vertical, parallel to spin axis), L = I omega must stay constant, forcing omega to rise. Quantitative: if I goes from 5 to 2 kg*m^2, omega goes from 2 to 5 rad/s. Energy is NOT conserved — skater does work pulling against centripetal forces of arms, so KE_rot increases from 10 J to 25 J (the body's chemical energy supplied 15 J).

Rolling without slipping: v_COM = R omega (m/s), where R is the rolling object's radius. Equivalently, a_COM = R alpha.
