The constraint that the contact point is momentarily at rest with respect to the surface (no sliding). AP FRQs (Science Practice 1: Modeling) require this constraint to couple translation and rotation: a wheel rolling down an incline has v_COM constrained to R omega throughout, allowing students to write a single equation in one unknown. Static friction (not kinetic) acts at the contact point — friction does NO work, since contact point has zero velocity instantaneously.

Total KE of a rolling object: KE = (1/2) m v_COM^2 + (1/2) I omega^2 (joules). Using rolling constraint v_COM = R omega: KE = (1/2)(m + I/R^2) v_COM^2 — equivalently KE = (1/2) m v_COM^2 (1 + I/(mR^2)). The factor (1 + I/(mR^2)) measures how much extra rotational KE accompanies a given translational v_COM. For a solid disk: 1 + 1/2 = 3/2, so KE_total = 3/2 of translation alone. AP FRQs (Science Practice 5: Calculations) chain this with energy conservation: m g h = (1/2)(m + I/R^2) v_COM^2.

On a ramp, smaller I/(m R^2) ratio means faster acceleration: a = g sin(theta) / (1 + I/(m R^2)). Race ranking (Science Practice 6: Argumentation): solid sphere (I/(mR^2) = 2/5, factor 7/5) > solid cylinder (1/2, factor 3/2) > hollow sphere (2/3, factor 5/3) > hollow cylinder/ring (1, factor 2). Frictionless block (no rotation) wins all: a = g sin(theta). AP FRQs commonly ask students to predict the winner without computing — the trick is to recognize that less rotational KE share means more KE for translation, hence higher v_COM at the bottom for the same h.

Static friction (NOT kinetic) acts on a rolling object that does not slip — the contact point is momentarily at rest relative to the surface. Static friction adjusts to whatever value is needed to maintain rolling, up to mu_s N. AP FRQs (Science Practice 6: Argumentation) catch students writing 'kinetic friction acts' — incorrect because no sliding occurs. Static friction does NO work (contact point velocity = 0, so W = F * 0 = 0), so mechanical energy is conserved on the rolling-down-incline problem. If theta exceeds critical angle (tan(theta) > 7 mu_s / 2 for solid sphere), static friction cannot supply enough torque, and sliding begins.

The contact point of a wheel rolling without slipping has INSTANTANEOUSLY zero velocity with respect to the ground. The wheel center moves at v_COM = R omega; the top of the wheel moves at 2 v_COM (twice as fast); the bottom contact at v_COM - R omega = 0. AP FRQs (Science Practice 6: Argumentation) test this: if the contact point had nonzero velocity, the wheel would be slipping (kinetic friction would act, doing negative work). The zero-velocity result explains why static friction (not kinetic) acts and why no energy is dissipated — a key Modeling step in rolling problems.

Total mechanical energy E_mech = KE + PE (joules). For pure translation: KE = (1/2) m v^2, PE = m g h or (1/2) k x^2. For rotation + translation: KE = (1/2) m v_COM^2 + (1/2) I omega^2, PE same as before. AP FRQs (Science Practice 5: Calculations) chain conservation: E_mech,i = E_mech,f when only conservative forces act. For rolling on incline: m g h = (1/2)(m + I/R^2) v_COM^2 (rotational KE plus translational KE, no friction-loss term because static friction does no work). When friction is present, mechanical energy decreases by f_k * d.

choices: Force doing zero net work in loop
questions.correctText: Force doing zero net work in loop
questions.explanation: A conservative force does zero net work over any closed loop. Equivalently, the work between two points is path-independent. Examples: gravity (W_g = -m g Delta y depends only on Delta y, not on path); spring force (W_s = -Delta((1/2)kx^2)); electrostatic forces. Each conservative force has an associated PE function with F = -dU/dx. Non-conservative forces (friction, drag, applied human forces) lack a PE and have path-dependent work. AP FRQ paragraph-length arguments (Science Practice 7) explicitly distinguish conservative vs non-conservative when invoking mechanical-energy conservation.

Kinetic friction converts mechanical energy into thermal energy at the contact surfaces — observable as temperature rise of the contact patches. Quantified: E_thermal = |W_f| = f_k * d_path (joules), where d_path is total distance slid. Joule's mid-19th-century paddle-wheel experiments established the mechanical equivalent (1 cal = 4.184 J). AP FRQs (Science Practice 5: Calculations) require explicit accounting: E_mech,f = E_mech,i - E_thermal. Paragraph-length arguments (Science Practice 7) about car brakes must trace the destination of the lost KE — brake pads heat, eventually radiating to surroundings, conserving total energy.

choices: v squared equals v_0 squared plus 2 a delta x
questions.explanation: Spring potential energy U_s = (1/2) k x^2 (joules), with k the spring constant (N/m) and x displacement from equilibrium (m). On the AP equation sheet. Always non-negative due to x^2. Hooke (1660s) discovered F = -k x; PE follows from integration. AP FRQs (Science Practice 5: Calculations) use this in conservation of mechanical energy for spring-launched carts: (1/2) k x_0^2 = (1/2) m v_max^2 at equilibrium, giving v_max = x_0 sqrt(k/m). For combined rotation + translation (a spring launching a rolling object), the factor (1 + I/(mR^2)) appears in the denominator of v_max^2.

choices: (m1 minus m2) g over (m1 plus m2)
questions.explanation: Rotational KE: KE_rot = (1/2) I omega^2 (joules), the rotational analog of (1/2) m v^2. Always non-negative. For a rigid body rotating about a fixed axis. For combined rotation + translation (e.g., rolling wheel): total KE = (1/2) m v_COM^2 + (1/2) I omega^2. AP FRQs (Science Practice 5: Calculations) chain KE_rot with energy conservation in rotating systems: a spinning flywheel storing KE_rot can later release it as work (industrial flywheels store ~MJ of energy). Also relevant to skater spin-up: KE_rot INCREASES (not decreases) when arms pull in, despite L being conserved.

Conservation of linear momentum: total p of an isolated system is constant when F_ext = 0. The cornerstone of all collision and explosion analysis. AP FRQs (Science Practice 5: Calculations) apply it across countless scenarios: car collisions, billiard-ball collisions, ballistic pendulums, rocket propulsion, recoil of guns. Paragraph-length arguments (Science Practice 7) require explicit identification of the system and justification of negligible external impulses. Combined with conservation of energy (when applicable — elastic only) gives two equations for solving 2-unknown collision problems.

Compound motion combines translation of the center of mass with rotation about it. Total KE = (1/2) m v_COM^2 + (1/2) I omega^2 (joules). AP FRQs (Science Practice 1: Modeling) require students to identify both translational v_COM and rotational omega, often linked by a rolling-without-slipping constraint v_COM = R omega. Common scenarios: a yo-yo descending (gravity does work converting to KE_trans + KE_rot); a ball thrown spinning (translation under gravity, rotation about COM independent); a frisbee in flight (rotation stabilizes against pitch via gyroscopic effect).

Total KE of an object in combined translation + rotation: KE = (1/2) m v_COM^2 + (1/2) I omega^2 (joules). For rolling without slipping, v_COM = R omega, so KE = (1/2)(m + I/R^2) v_COM^2. AP FRQs (Science Practice 5: Calculations) use this in conservation of energy for rolling objects on inclines, in pulley systems with mass, in yo-yo problems. The translational + rotational decomposition (König's theorem) is exact for rigid bodies and underlies all combined-motion analysis at the AP level. Linear analog: KE_trans alone for point particles.

For a two-body rotating system (e.g., a binary star or two atoms in a diatomic molecule), one can use the reduced mass mu = m_1 m_2 / (m_1 + m_2) to reduce the two-body problem to a single body at radius r_rel from a fixed center. AP Physics 1 typically avoids the formal reduced-mass formalism, but the idea appears qualitatively when students analyze orbits or rotations of pairs. Newton (Principia, 1687) showed two-body gravitational orbits reduce to motion of reduced mass mu in a central field, justifying treating the Earth-Sun system as Earth orbiting a fixed Sun (Sun's much larger mass makes mu ≈ m_Earth).

When ONLY internal forces act on a system, the center of mass continues at constant velocity (Newton's first law for COM). Internal forces cancel pairwise by Newton's third law and cannot accelerate the COM. AP FRQs (Science Practice 7: Paragraph-Length Argument) use this powerful insight on explosions: a 10 kg shell traveling at 50 m/s explodes; despite the dramatic internal forces, the COM continues at 50 m/s. Fragments redistribute around this COM motion. For free fall, the COM has a = -g regardless of internal rotations or fragmentations — exemplified by a wrench tossed across a frictionless table, whose COM moves linearly while the wrench rotates.

Simple harmonic motion (SHM): motion under a linear restoring force F = -k x, with displacement x from equilibrium. Solutions are sinusoidal: x(t) = A cos(omega t + phi), where omega = sqrt(k/m). Hooke's Law (1660s) provides the linear restoring force; Newton's second law plus this force yields the second-order ODE m d^2x/dt^2 = -k x with sinusoidal solutions. AP FRQs (Science Practice 1: Modeling) require students to identify the restoring force, verify proportionality to displacement, and confirm SHM behavior before applying period formulas. Examples: mass on spring, small-angle pendulum, vibrating molecule.

Hooke's Law: F = -k x (newtons), where k is the spring constant (N/m) and x is displacement from equilibrium (m). Discovered by Robert Hooke (1660s). The negative sign indicates restoring direction (toward equilibrium). On the AP equation sheet. Linear in x — small-amplitude approximation valid for real springs within their elastic limit. AP FRQs (Science Practice 5: Calculations) chain Hooke's Law with Newton's second to derive omega = sqrt(k/m) and T = 2 pi sqrt(m/k). Stiffer spring (larger k) gives faster oscillation; heavier mass gives slower. Pendulum analog (small-angle): F = -m g (x/L).

choices: (m1 minus m2) g over (m1 plus m2)
questions.explanation: Spring constant k unit: N/m (newtons per meter) — the force per unit displacement. A k = 100 N/m spring requires 100 N to stretch 1 m or 1 N to stretch 1 cm. AP FRQs (Science Practice 4: Experimental Design) often ask students to measure k by hanging known weights and recording extension: k = m g / x. Then verify the predicted T = 2 pi sqrt(m/k) by timing oscillations. Practical magnitudes: a slinky has k ~ 0.5 N/m (very soft); a car suspension spring k ~ 50,000 N/m; an atomic bond modeled as a spring k ~ 100-1000 N/m.

Amplitude A (meters) is the maximum displacement from equilibrium in SHM. The position oscillates between +A and -A. Note: T (period) and omega (angular frequency) are independent of A for ideal SHM — a key Galilean-isochronism property tested on AP FRQs. AP FRQs (Science Practice 6: Argumentation) test this isochronism: doubling A doubles v_max but does NOT change T (since omega = sqrt(k/m) does not contain A). Total energy E_total = (1/2) k A^2 — quadratic in A. v_max = A omega = A sqrt(k/m), reached at equilibrium where all energy is KE.

Period T (seconds) is the time for one complete oscillation cycle. For mass-on-spring: T = 2 pi sqrt(m/k). For small-angle pendulum: T = 2 pi sqrt(L/g). On the AP equation sheet. T = 1/f = 2 pi / omega. AP FRQs (Science Practice 6: Argumentation) test Galilean isochronism: T does NOT depend on amplitude (for ideal SHM). T does depend on m and k for springs; on L and g for pendulums. Earth's gravity pendulum (L = 1 m): T ≈ 2.0 s. On the moon (g/6), same pendulum has T ≈ 4.9 s. Galileo discovered pendulum isochronism c.1602 by timing chandelier swings against his pulse.

Frequency f (Hz = 1/s) is the number of complete oscillation cycles per second. Reciprocal of period: f = 1/T. Related to angular frequency: omega = 2 pi f (rad/s). AP FRQs (Science Practice 5: Calculations) connect f to physical parameters: for mass-on-spring f = (1/(2 pi)) sqrt(k/m); for pendulum f = (1/(2 pi)) sqrt(g/L). Typical magnitudes: a tuning fork at 440 Hz (concert A); a quartz crystal at 32,768 Hz (digital watch); piano middle C at 261.63 Hz. Heinrich Hertz (1857-1894) gave name to the unit after his work proving electromagnetic waves.

Hertz (Hz) = 1 cycle / second = 1/s. Named after Heinrich Hertz (1857-1894), who experimentally demonstrated radio waves (1887) and confirmed Maxwell's electromagnetic theory. AP FRQs (Science Practice 2: Mathematical Routines) require unit consistency: f (Hz) = 1/T (1/s) must check dimensionally. Practical magnitudes: alpha brain waves 8-13 Hz; a hummingbird wing flap ~50 Hz; AM radio 0.5-1.7 MHz; visible light ~5 * 10^14 Hz; gamma rays > 10^19 Hz. Hertz's spark-gap experiments established the unity of EM waves and light, a foundation Einstein later extended with photons.

Mass-on-spring period: T = 2 pi sqrt(m/k) (seconds), with m in kg and k in N/m. On the AP equation sheet. Independent of amplitude (Galilean isochronism). Derived from Hooke's Law F = -k x plus Newton's second m a = -k x, giving angular frequency omega = sqrt(k/m). AP FRQs (Science Practice 5: Calculations) commonly chain this with: a 0.5 kg mass on a 200 N/m spring has T = 2 pi sqrt(0.5/200) = 2 pi (0.05) ≈ 0.314 s, f ≈ 3.18 Hz. Limiting cases: very stiff spring (large k) gives very short T; very massive load (large m) gives long T. m and k are the ONLY parameters.

Small-angle pendulum period: T = 2 pi sqrt(L/g) (seconds), with L in m and g in m/s^2. On the AP equation sheet. Independent of mass and (for small theta) of amplitude (Galilean isochronism). Derived from small-angle approximation sin(theta) ≈ theta: restoring force ≈ -m g (x/L), giving omega = sqrt(g/L). For L = 1 m on Earth (g = 9.8): T = 2 pi sqrt(1/9.8) ≈ 2.0 s. AP FRQs (Science Practice 4: Experimental Design) often ask students to verify the formula by varying L and timing T. Limiting case: vary g (different planets) and predict T_new = T_Earth sqrt(g_Earth / g_new).

Angular frequency omega = 2 pi f = 2 pi / T (rad/s). Connects oscillation frequency to circular-motion analog: a point moving in a circle of radius A at omega traces sinusoidal x(t) = A cos(omega t) on one axis. On the AP equation sheet. AP FRQs (Science Practice 2: Mathematical Routines) chain omega with physical parameters: omega = sqrt(k/m) for spring; omega = sqrt(g/L) for pendulum. Connects to position equation x(t) = A cos(omega t + phi) and velocity v(t) = -A omega sin(omega t + phi). The factor 2 pi arises because radians measure full circle as 2 pi, not 360.

Total energy in SHM: E_total = (1/2) k A^2 (joules), where A is amplitude. Constant throughout oscillation (no friction). Energy oscillates between KE and PE: at equilibrium (x = 0), all is KE = (1/2) m v_max^2; at maximum displacement (x = ±A), all is PE = (1/2) k A^2. AP FRQs (Science Practice 5: Calculations) chain this with v_max = A omega = A sqrt(k/m): substituting confirms (1/2) m v_max^2 = (1/2) k A^2. Quadratic in A: doubling amplitude quadruples total energy. Useful AP problem: amplitude after partially inelastic collision adds mass, reducing E_total but conserving p.

Kinetic energy is maximum at equilibrium (x = 0) in SHM, because all energy is KE at that point (PE = 0 if we set zero at equilibrium). v_max = A omega = A sqrt(k/m). AP FRQs (Science Practice 6: Argumentation) often ask students to predict KE distribution across cycle: at x = ±A, KE = 0 (object momentarily at rest at turning points); at x = ±A/2, KE = (1/2)(k A^2)(1 - 1/4) = (3/8) k A^2 (75% of max KE); at x = 0, KE = (1/2) k A^2 (100%). Used in spring-launched cart problems where the spring releases all PE as KE at equilibrium.

Potential energy is maximum at the turning points (x = ±A) in SHM, because all energy is PE at those instants (v = 0 momentarily). PE_max = (1/2) k A^2 = E_total. The object reverses direction here. AP FRQs (Science Practice 5: Calculations) use this for energy bookkeeping: between equilibrium and turning point, KE → PE; between turning point and equilibrium, PE → KE. At x = ±A/2: PE = (1/2)(k)(A/2)^2 = (1/8) k A^2 (25% of max PE). The transformation continues sinusoidally throughout each cycle, with frequency 2 * omega (energy passes through max twice per cycle).

Spring PE U_s = (1/2) k x^2 (joules), with k the spring constant (N/m) and x displacement from equilibrium (m). On the AP equation sheet. Always non-negative due to x^2. Quadratic in x: doubling compression quadruples stored PE. Hooke's Law F = -k x integrates to U_s via W_cons = -Delta U. AP FRQs (Science Practice 5: Calculations) chain this with energy conservation in spring-mass systems: at any x, total energy E = (1/2) m v^2 + (1/2) k x^2 = (1/2) k A^2 (constant). v(x) = omega sqrt(A^2 - x^2) — sinusoidal velocity confirmed.

At equilibrium (x = 0) in SHM, ALL energy is kinetic: KE = (1/2) m v_max^2 = (1/2) k A^2. PE is zero (with conventional choice of reference at equilibrium). At x = ±A, all energy is PE: KE = 0, PE = (1/2) k A^2. Energy oscillates between these forms at frequency 2 * omega (twice per oscillation cycle). AP FRQs (Science Practice 6: Argumentation) test this conservation: a mass-on-spring released from rest at x = A has v = 0, all PE; at x = 0 all energy is KE giving v_max = A sqrt(k/m); at x = -A again all PE, v = 0, then back.

A simple pendulum: a point mass m on a massless inextensible string of length L, swinging in a vertical plane. Restoring force is the tangential component of gravity: F_t = -m g sin(theta). For small theta (< 15 degrees), sin(theta) ≈ theta, giving SHM with T = 2 pi sqrt(L/g). Galileo discovered isochronism c.1602 by timing chandelier swings against his pulse; Huygens (1656) used pendulums to make the first accurate clocks. AP FRQs (Science Practice 1: Modeling) require the small-angle approximation to be stated explicitly, since beyond 15 degrees the period deviates noticeably from the formula.

Small-angle approximation: sin(theta) ≈ theta (radians), valid for theta < ~15 degrees with < 1% error. Required to linearize the pendulum equation of motion and obtain SHM with T = 2 pi sqrt(L/g). For larger amplitudes, T increases slightly — for theta_0 = 30 degrees, T is ~2% longer; for theta_0 = 90 degrees, T is ~18% longer. AP FRQs (Science Practice 6: Argumentation) sometimes ask students to identify when the small-angle approximation breaks down. Real-world pendulum clocks use small amplitudes (~3-5 degrees) to maintain timekeeping accuracy. Galileo discovered the limit empirically.

Pendulum period T = 2 pi sqrt(L/g) is INDEPENDENT of mass m. This Galilean-equivalence-principle result follows because m appears in BOTH the restoring force (m g sin theta) AND the inertia (m) on opposite sides of F = m a, so it cancels. Galileo demonstrated this by simultaneously dropping a heavy stone and a light feather (apocryphal Leaning Tower experiment); Hammer-and-feather on the Apollo 15 moon walk (1971) gave the modern televised confirmation. AP FRQs (Science Practice 6: Argumentation) require students to identify this mass-independence and explain it via the equivalence principle (gravitational mass = inertial mass).

Pendulum period depends ONLY on L (length) and g (local gravity): T = 2 pi sqrt(L/g). Longer L = longer T (slower); larger g = shorter T (faster). On Earth (g = 9.8 m/s^2), L = 1 m gives T ≈ 2.0 s. On the moon (g/6), same pendulum has T ≈ 4.9 s. AP FRQs (Science Practice 4: Experimental Design) use pendulums to measure g: T^2 = (4 pi^2 / g) L, so plotting T^2 vs L gives a line of slope 4 pi^2 / g, from which g is extracted. Pendulum geodesy used in 18th-19th centuries to map Earth's variable gravity.

A longer pendulum has a LONGER period: T = 2 pi sqrt(L/g) scales with sqrt(L). Doubling L increases T by factor sqrt(2) ≈ 1.41. Quadrupling L doubles T. AP FRQs (Science Practice 4: Data Analysis) chain this in graph problems: students plot T vs L on log-log axes to extract the exponent (1/2). For a grandfather clock pendulum with T = 2 s (one-second tick each half-cycle), L ≈ 0.99 m on Earth — the canonical 'seconds pendulum.' Tuning a pendulum clock involves micro-adjusting L to match a reference frequency.

Resonance occurs when a driving force frequency matches a system's natural frequency, causing very large amplitude buildup (limited only by damping). Galileo first studied resonance in pendulums; Hertz used it in spark-gap radio circuits. AP FRQs (Science Practice 6: Argumentation) classic example: Tacoma Narrows Bridge collapse (1940) — wind-induced oscillations matched the bridge's torsional natural frequency. Other examples: pushing a child on a swing in time with each swing (rocketing amplitude); shattering a wine glass with a sung tone matching its natural mode; MRI machines tuning radio waves to nuclear precession frequencies.

choices: 2 m_1 m_2 g over (m_1 plus m_2)
questions.explanation: Natural (resonant) frequency f_0 = (1/(2 pi)) sqrt(k_eff / m_eff) — the frequency at which a system oscillates when displaced from equilibrium and released, with no external driving. For mass-on-spring: f_0 = (1/(2 pi)) sqrt(k/m). For pendulum: f_0 = (1/(2 pi)) sqrt(g/L). AP FRQs (Science Practice 6: Argumentation) test the resonance condition: when external driving frequency f_drive = f_0, amplitude grows dramatically — the basis of musical instruments tuning to standardized pitches and of LC radio-receiver circuits selecting specific stations.

Damping: progressive amplitude reduction due to dissipative forces (friction, air drag, viscous losses). Light damping reduces amplitude exponentially: A(t) = A_0 exp(-gamma t). Critical damping returns the system to equilibrium in the shortest time without oscillation (used in car shock absorbers and door closers). Overdamped systems return slowly without oscillation. AP FRQs (Science Practice 6: Argumentation) discuss damping qualitatively at the AP Physics 1 level — students should identify that a swinging pendulum eventually stops because of air friction at the pivot and air drag on the bob, converting mechanical energy to thermal energy.

Driven oscillation: an oscillator subject to a periodic external force. Steady-state amplitude depends on the driving frequency f_drive relative to natural f_0: maximum amplitude at f_drive = f_0 (resonance). With damping, the resonance peak is finite-height. Hertz (1887) used driven LC circuits to generate radio waves matching antenna resonance. AP FRQs (Science Practice 6: Argumentation) test the qualitative response: amplitude grows when driving matches natural, falls when driving differs. Engineering examples: tuning forks (resonance amplifies the sound); musical instruments (resonator amplifies string vibrations at characteristic pitches).

The Tacoma Narrows Bridge (Washington state) collapsed on November 7, 1940, four months after opening. Wind-induced torsional oscillations grew dramatically until the suspension bridge tore itself apart. Originally attributed to simple resonance, modern analysis identifies aeroelastic flutter (a more complex feedback between airflow and structure). The footage remains the most-cited textbook example of structural resonance — including in AP Physics 1 paragraph-length arguments (Science Practice 7) about engineering design to avoid mechanical resonance. Modern bridges (Golden Gate retrofit, Millennium Bridge London 2000) use tuned mass dampers to suppress dangerous modes.

Density rho = m / V (kg/m^3) — mass per unit volume. Water at 4 °C: rho = 1000 kg/m^3 (1 g/cm^3) — the SI standard. Air at sea level: rho = 1.225 kg/m^3 (~1000x less dense than water). Mercury: rho = 13,600 kg/m^3. AP FRQs (Science Practice 5: Calculations) chain density with buoyancy (F_b = rho_fluid V_displaced g) and pressure (P = rho g h). Density depends on temperature (water expands when heated, becoming less dense) and pressure (negligible for liquids in AP problems; large for gases — see ideal gas law in AP Physics 2 Unit 2).

Pressure P = F / A (Pa = N/m^2), force exerted perpendicular to a surface divided by the contact area. Pascal (1647) studied pressure in static fluids and gave name to the SI unit. Pressure is scalar in static fluids (acts equally in all directions at a point) — this is Pascal's principle. AP FRQs (Science Practice 5: Calculations) compute pressure on submarine windows, the bottom of a deep dive (~10 atm at 90 m), and atmospheric column weights (P_atm = 1.013 * 10^5 Pa = weight of air column above per unit area). The vector force on a surface is F = P A, perpendicular to surface.

Pascal (Pa) = 1 N/m^2 = 1 kg / (m * s^2). Named after Blaise Pascal (1623-1662), who established the principles of fluid pressure and invented the barometer-by-proxy experiments showing atmospheric pressure decreases with altitude. AP FRQs (Science Practice 2: Mathematical Routines) require unit consistency: P = rho g h with rho in kg/m^3, g in m/s^2, h in m gives Pa automatically. Practical magnitudes: atmospheric pressure ~10^5 Pa; tire pressure ~2 * 10^5 Pa (gauge) = 3 * 10^5 Pa (absolute); blood pressure ~10^4 Pa (gauge, ~100 mmHg systolic). 1 atm = 101,325 Pa.

Hydrostatic pressure: P(h) = P_0 + rho g h (Pa), where h is depth below the surface, rho is fluid density, g is gravitational acceleration, and P_0 is the surface pressure (typically atmospheric ~10^5 Pa). Pascal's hydrostatic equation. On the AP equation sheet. Linear in depth — doubling h doubles the gauge pressure (excess over atmospheric). AP FRQs (Science Practice 5: Calculations) test: at 10 m depth in water, P_gauge = (1000)(9.8)(10) = 9.8 * 10^4 Pa ≈ 1 atm. So total absolute P ≈ 2 atm. Submarines withstand 30+ atm at 300 m. Derived from F = m g of a fluid column over area A: P = m g / A = rho V g / A = rho A h g / A = rho g h.

Standard atmospheric pressure: P_atm = 1.013 * 10^5 Pa = 101,325 Pa = 1 atm = 760 mmHg = 14.7 psi. The weight of the air column above per unit area at sea level on Earth. Torricelli (1643) discovered atmospheric pressure with his mercury-column barometer; Pascal sent his brother-in-law up a mountain (Puy-de-Dôme, 1648) to verify pressure decreases with altitude. AP FRQs (Science Practice 6: Argumentation) test: a piston exposed to vacuum on one side experiences ~10^5 N per m^2 from atmospheric pressure on the other — explaining 'suction cups' and the difficulty of opening valves against atmospheric pressure differentials.

Buoyancy F_b is the upward force exerted by a fluid on any submerged object, arising from the pressure difference between deeper (higher P) and shallower (lower P) parts of the object. Magnitude given by Archimedes' principle: F_b = rho_fluid V_displaced g (newtons). Archimedes (c. 250 BCE) discovered this while bathing — the 'Eureka!' moment, allegedly. AP FRQs (Science Practice 5: Calculations) chain buoyancy with weight for floating/sinking analysis: if F_b > F_g, object accelerates upward; if F_b < F_g, downward; F_b = F_g means floating at equilibrium (no acceleration).

Archimedes' principle: the buoyant force equals the weight of the fluid displaced by the submerged object. F_b = rho_fluid V_disp g. Discovered by Archimedes (c. 250 BCE) when testing whether King Hieron's crown was pure gold. On the AP equation sheet. Independent of the object's density (depends only on fluid density and displaced volume). AP FRQs (Science Practice 6: Argumentation) classic: a steel ship floats despite steel being 8x denser than water, because the ship's hull encloses a large displaced volume of water, achieving F_b ≥ F_g. A solid steel cube of the same mass would sink because V_disp is too small.

choices: x equals x_0 plus v_0 t plus half a t squared
questions.explanation: A floating object is in equilibrium: F_b = F_g, so rho_fluid V_displaced g = m g, giving V_displaced = m / rho_fluid. The fraction of object volume submerged: V_disp / V_object = rho_object / rho_fluid. Ice in water: rho_ice / rho_water = 917/1000 ≈ 0.917, so ~92% submerged — confirmed by the proverbial 'tip of the iceberg.' AP FRQs (Science Practice 5: Calculations) compute submerged fractions for various density combinations. A boat carrying cargo settles deeper (more V_disp) until new F_b matches new (greater) total weight.

questions.prompt: Object sinking when this is true.
questions.explanation: An object sinks when its average density exceeds the fluid density: rho_object > rho_fluid. Even when fully submerged, F_b = rho_fluid V_object g < m g = rho_object V_object g, so net force is downward. AP FRQs (Science Practice 5: Calculations): a steel ball (rho = 7850 kg/m^3) sinks in water (1000 kg/m^3) because rho_steel > rho_water. Same steel hollowed into a hull (with internal air at rho_air ~1 kg/m^3) achieves average rho_avg < rho_water, allowing it to float. Submarines control rho_avg by flooding/emptying ballast tanks — buoyancy engineering.

A ship floats by displacing a volume of water whose weight equals the ship's total weight. The hull encloses air at low density, so the ship's average density (steel hull + air interior + cargo) is less than water's density (1000 kg/m^3). When loaded, the ship sinks deeper, displacing more water until new F_b matches new total weight. Plimsoll lines on cargo ships indicate maximum safe loading for various water densities (fresh, salt, tropical, etc.). AP FRQs (Science Practice 6: Argumentation) test this with paragraph-length arguments about why hollow steel ships float while solid steel sinks — same metal, different average density.

Pascal's principle (1647): in a confined incompressible fluid at rest, pressure applied at one point is transmitted undiminished to every other point. Blaise Pascal demonstrated this experimentally. Foundation of hydraulic systems. On the AP equation sheet. AP FRQs (Science Practice 5: Calculations) use Pascal's principle in hydraulic-press problems: a small piston (area A_1) under force F_1 produces pressure P_1 = F_1/A_1, transmitted through the connecting fluid to a large piston (area A_2 >> A_1), where the lifting force is F_2 = P_1 A_2 = F_1 (A_2/A_1). Mechanical advantage A_2/A_1 makes hydraulic jacks lift cars.

Hydraulic press: two connected fluid-filled chambers with pistons of different areas. A small force F_1 on the small piston (area A_1) creates pressure P = F_1/A_1, transmitted (Pascal's principle) to the large piston (area A_2). Output force F_2 = P A_2 = F_1 (A_2/A_1) — mechanical advantage A_2/A_1. AP FRQs (Science Practice 5: Calculations): a car-jack hydraulic lift with A_2/A_1 = 100 can lift 1000 kg car with only 98 N input. Distance trade-off: small piston moves 100x further than large piston (volume conservation V_1 = V_2 gives A_1 d_1 = A_2 d_2). Energy conserved: F_1 d_1 = F_2 d_2.

Mechanical advantage in a hydraulic press = A_2 / A_1 (ratio of output to input piston areas). Force amplification F_2 = F_1 (A_2/A_1). AP FRQs (Science Practice 5: Calculations): a 200:1 area ratio lifts a 20,000 N car with only 100 N input force. The cost (Science Practice 6: Argumentation): the small piston must travel 200x farther — energy is conserved, since F_1 d_1 = F_2 d_2 ⟹ both sides are work W. Hydraulics multiply force at the expense of distance, analogous to mechanical levers (Archimedes' lever principle). Used in car jacks, brake systems, construction equipment.

Volume is conserved in a hydraulic system because the working fluid (oil, water) is incompressible: V_1 = V_2 → A_1 d_1 = A_2 d_2, where d_1, d_2 are piston displacements. Combined with Pascal's principle F_2/F_1 = A_2/A_1, this yields F_1 d_1 = F_2 d_2 = W (work conserved). AP FRQs (Science Practice 5: Calculations) test the trade-off explicitly: a 100x force amplification requires 100x distance reduction at the output piston. Energy conservation is intact: input work equals output work (neglecting friction in the system). This is mechanical advantage at the cost of distance — Archimedes' lever principle generalized.

In a hydraulic system, the smaller (input) piston travels a longer distance than the larger (output) piston, in inverse proportion to area: d_1 / d_2 = A_2 / A_1. For a 100:1 area ratio, the input piston moves 100x farther while exerting 100x less force, with energy conservation: F_1 d_1 = F_2 d_2 = W. AP FRQs (Science Practice 6: Argumentation) test this in paragraph-length arguments: students must explain why hydraulic force amplification is NOT a violation of energy conservation — the trade-off is distance. This is analogous to a long lever arm amplifying torque while requiring more arc length traveled.

Continuity equation: A_1 v_1 = A_2 v_2 (m^3/s) for incompressible fluid in steady flow through a tube. Conservation of volume flow rate Q = A v. On the AP equation sheet. AP FRQs (Science Practice 5: Calculations) apply this to garden hoses (nozzle narrows, water speed increases), river constrictions (narrow gorge = faster current), and blood-vessel narrowings (atherosclerosis). For incompressible flow (liquids, air at low speeds), rho stays constant; for compressible flow, rho A v is conserved (mass flow rate). Derived from conservation of mass: in a steady pipe, what enters at section 1 must exit at section 2.

Bernoulli's equation: P + (1/2) rho v^2 + rho g h = constant along a streamline for incompressible, inviscid, steady flow. Daniel Bernoulli (1738) derived it from conservation of energy applied to a fluid element. On the AP equation sheet. AP FRQs (Science Practice 5: Calculations) chain it with continuity (A v = constant) to solve for pressure differences in narrowing pipes, lift on airplane wings, and water-tower flow. Three forms of fluid energy per unit volume: pressure (P), kinetic ((1/2) rho v^2), and gravitational (rho g h). Conservation: faster flow → lower pressure (the Bernoulli effect).

Bernoulli effect: pressure is lower where fluid speed is higher (along the same streamline at the same height). Direct consequence of Bernoulli's equation P + (1/2) rho v^2 = constant (for horizontal flow). Demonstrated by Daniel Bernoulli (1738). AP FRQs (Science Practice 6: Argumentation) classic applications: airfoils generate lift because air moves faster over the curved top surface than the flatter bottom, creating lower pressure above and net upward force; spinning baseballs curve because the surface drags air around, asymmetrically accelerating flow and creating pressure imbalance (Magnus effect); a vacuum-cleaner nozzle reduces pressure for suction.

Airfoil lift: air moves faster over the curved upper wing surface than the flatter lower surface (continuity + airfoil shape), so by Bernoulli's effect the upper pressure is LOWER than the lower pressure, creating a net upward force = (P_lower - P_upper) * Wing_area. AP FRQs (Science Practice 6: Argumentation) require students to explicitly invoke both continuity (which sets the speed difference) and Bernoulli (which relates speed to pressure). Note: simple Bernoulli explanation is incomplete in detail — full lift theory requires Kutta condition and circulation — but Physics 1 stays at the Bernoulli level. Lift balances gravity in level flight; lift exceeds weight in climb.

Two conservation laws in incompressible-inviscid fluid flow: (1) Mass conservation, expressed as the continuity equation A v = constant (volume flow rate constant for incompressible). (2) Energy conservation, expressed as Bernoulli's equation P + (1/2) rho v^2 + rho g h = constant along a streamline. AP FRQs (Science Practice 5: Calculations) chain them: continuity gives the speed at a narrowing; Bernoulli converts that to pressure. Together they handle most AP fluid-flow problems. Real flows also have viscous dissipation (modeled by Poiseuille's law for tubes), but AP Physics 1 stays inviscid for fluid-flow analyses.

Viscosity (eta or mu) is a measure of a fluid's internal resistance to shear flow — fluid friction between adjacent fluid layers. Newton's law of viscosity: shear stress = eta * (velocity gradient). Newton (1687) and Stokes (1851) developed the formal theory. AP Physics 1 typically treats viscosity qualitatively. AP FRQs (Science Practice 6: Argumentation) ask students to compare viscosities qualitatively: water (low viscosity, flows readily) vs. honey (high viscosity, flows slowly). Viscosity decreases with temperature for liquids (hot honey flows faster), increases with temperature for gases (rare comparison). SI unit: Pa * s (pascal-second).

Water has low viscosity (eta = 1.0 * 10^-3 Pa * s at 20 °C) compared to many liquids. Air viscosity is even lower (1.8 * 10^-5 Pa * s). AP FRQs (Science Practice 6: Argumentation) compare fluid behaviors: low-viscosity fluids flow readily, form turbulent regimes at moderate speeds (Reynolds number Re = rho v L / eta becomes large for low eta), and minimize drag at low-Re regimes. Water's low viscosity allows efficient pipe flow, swimming, and rainfall dynamics. Inviscid (zero-viscosity) idealization underlies Bernoulli's equation — real water is approximately inviscid for AP-level problems.

Honey has high viscosity (eta ~ 10 Pa * s at room temperature) — about 10,000x more viscous than water. Other high-viscosity examples: motor oil (eta ~ 0.5 Pa * s), molasses, glycerin. AP FRQs (Science Practice 6: Argumentation) classic: pour water and honey side-by-side from cups — water flows readily, honey 'pitches' slowly. High viscosity dominates at low Reynolds numbers, where viscous forces exceed inertial forces (creeping flow). Heating honey lowers viscosity (more flowable). Pitch-drop experiment at the University of Queensland (1927-present) records pitch — extremely high-viscosity tar — dropping ~once per decade.

choices: v_0 squared sin 2 theta over g
questions.explanation: Laminar flow: fluid moves in parallel layers (streamlines) without lateral mixing. Stable, predictable, smooth. Reynolds number Re = rho v L / eta < ~2300 for pipe flow. Bernoulli's equation strictly applies to laminar inviscid flow. AP FRQs (Science Practice 6: Argumentation) classic: water slowly draining from a faucet shows laminar flow (smooth, transparent stream); high-velocity faucet flow becomes turbulent (chaotic, bubbly). Laminar flow has lower drag than turbulent at the same velocity; engineers minimize Re to maintain laminar flow in aircraft wings and pipelines. Osborne Reynolds (1883) experimentally distinguished laminar vs turbulent.

Turbulent flow: chaotic eddies and lateral mixing of fluid. High Reynolds number Re = rho v L / eta > ~4000 for pipe flow. AP FRQs (Science Practice 6: Argumentation) qualitatively compare: a slowly-running faucet flows laminar (smooth stream); opening it fully causes turbulent (foaming) flow. Turbulence drives the largest unsolved problem in classical physics — the Navier-Stokes turbulence problem is one of the Millennium Prize Problems. Practical effects: turbulent flow has higher drag and pressure losses than laminar (planes designed to keep wing flow laminar); turbulent mixing enables combustion and atmospheric weather dynamics.

F_net = m a (newtons = kg * m/s^2) — Newton's second law from Principia (1687). Net external force vector equals mass times acceleration vector. On the AP equation sheet. The central equation of AP Physics 1 mechanics, applied in every dynamics FRQ (Science Practice 5: Calculations). For variable mass: F = dp/dt is more general. Equilibrium: F_net = 0 ⟹ a = 0 (translational equilibrium, constant v or rest). AP FRQs require free-body diagrams (Science Practice 1: Modeling) followed by component-wise application: F_{net,x} = m a_x and F_{net,y} = m a_y.

Kinetic energy KE = (1/2) m v^2 (joules). Always non-negative; scalar. Doubling v quadruples KE. Connected to work via the work-energy theorem: W_net = Delta KE. For rotation: KE_rot = (1/2) I omega^2. For rolling without slipping: total KE = (1/2)(m + I/R^2) v_COM^2. Coriolis (1829) and Leibniz (1686) developed the modern form. AP FRQs (Science Practice 5: Calculations) chain KE with conservation of mechanical energy (KE + PE = constant when only conservative forces act) on roller coasters, pendulums, and inclines. On the AP equation sheet.

Momentum p = m v (kg * m/s) — vector with direction of velocity. On the AP equation sheet. Conserved in isolated systems (F_ext = 0). Newton originally wrote his second law as F_net = dp/dt. AP FRQs (Science Practice 5: Calculations) apply conservation of momentum to all collisions and explosions: m_1 v_{1,i} + m_2 v_{2,i} = m_1 v_{1,f} + m_2 v_{2,f}. Vector nature is critical: in 2D collisions, x and y components conserve independently. Paragraph-length arguments (Science Practice 7) require explicit justification of 'F_ext = 0' before invoking conservation.

Torque tau = r F sin(theta) (N * m) — the rotational analog of force, what causes angular acceleration: tau_net = I alpha. Vector along rotation axis (right-hand rule). On the AP equation sheet. Archimedes (c. 250 BCE) understood torque qualitatively via the lever principle. AP FRQs (Science Practice 5: Calculations) require computing each torque acting on an extended body (with sign — CCW positive convention), summing for tau_net, then applying tau_net = I alpha. Equilibrium requires tau_net = 0 in addition to F_net = 0. Maximum tau when force is perpendicular to position vector (theta = 90).

choices: (m1 minus m2) g over (m1 plus m2)
questions.explanation: Friction (newtons) is a contact force parallel to surfaces opposing relative motion (kinetic, f_k = mu_k N) or impending motion (static, f_s up to mu_s N). Coulomb (1785) systematized friction laws. AP FRQs (Science Practice 1: Modeling) require explicit friction force vectors on every free-body diagram, directed opposite velocity or impending motion. Friction does negative work on moving objects (W_f = -f_k * d), converting mechanical energy to thermal. On an incline: f_k = mu_k mg cos(theta). Static friction on rolling-without-slipping objects does ZERO work (contact point has zero velocity).

Kinetic energy KE = (1/2) m v^2 (joules). Scalar, always non-negative, scales quadratically with v. AP FRQs (Science Practice 5: Calculations) chain KE with the work-energy theorem (W_net = Delta KE) and with conservation of mechanical energy (KE + PE = constant when only conservative forces act). For rotation: KE_rot = (1/2) I omega^2; total for rolling: KE = (1/2) m v_COM^2 + (1/2) I omega^2. On the AP equation sheet. Doubling v quadruples KE — explaining why stopping distances scale with v^2 for cars with constant braking friction.

Potential energy U (joules) — stored energy from position in a conservative-force field. Gravitational: U_g = m g h. Spring: U_s = (1/2) k x^2. Scalar but with sign depending on chosen reference. Term coined by Rankine (1853). On the AP equation sheet. AP FRQs (Science Practice 1: Modeling) require students to declare a reference level (U_g = 0 location) before computing U. Conservation: KE + PE = constant when only conservative forces act. Non-conservative forces (friction) drain mechanical energy to thermal — accounted via energy bookkeeping E_mech,f = E_mech,i - |W_friction|.

Work-Energy Theorem: W_net = Delta KE = KE_f - KE_i (joules). Derived by integrating F = m a over displacement. On the AP equation sheet. AP FRQs (Science Practice 5: Calculations) apply this to find final speed when forces are non-uniform — spring-launched carts, variable-force scenarios — bypassing the need to solve F = m a directly. Linear analog of the rotational work-KE theorem: W_rot = Delta KE_rot using W_rot = tau * theta. Powerful problem-solving tool when forces vary over distance but final KE is what's needed.

questions.prompt: Force doing no net work in loop.
questions.explanation: A conservative force does zero net work over any closed loop — its work is path-independent. Examples: gravity, spring forces, electrostatic forces. Each conservative force has an associated PE function with F = -dU/dx. Non-conservative forces (friction, drag, applied human forces) are path-dependent. AP FRQ paragraph-length arguments (Science Practice 7) require explicit identification of conservative vs non-conservative before invoking mechanical-energy conservation. Only conservative forces contribute to PE; non-conservative forces drain mechanical energy to thermal.

Friction converts mechanical energy into thermal energy at contact surfaces: E_thermal = |W_friction| = f_k * d_path (joules). Joule (1840s) established the mechanical equivalent of heat (1 cal = 4.184 J) through paddle-wheel experiments — confirming conservation of total energy. AP FRQs (Science Practice 5: Calculations) require explicit thermal-energy accounting in problems with friction: E_mech,f = E_mech,i - E_thermal. Paragraph-length arguments (Science Practice 7) about car braking trace the destination: brake pads heat up, eventually radiating to surroundings, conserving total energy.

Angular momentum L = I omega (kg * m^2 / s) for a rigid body — the rotational analog of linear momentum. For a point mass: L = m v r sin(theta). Vector along rotation axis (right-hand rule). Conserved when net external torque is zero (tau_ext = 0). AP FRQs (Science Practice 5: Calculations) chain conservation to explain skater spin-up (I decreases, omega increases), pulsar formation (I drops by factor 10^10), and Kepler's second law (equal areas in equal times — orbital L conservation). On the AP equation sheet. Newton-Euler reformulation of mechanics extended to rotation.

Moment of inertia I (kg * m^2) — rotational analog of mass. Resistance to angular acceleration: tau_net = I alpha. Depends on mass distribution AND axis of rotation. Point mass: I = m r^2. Solid sphere about center: (2/5) m R^2. Solid cylinder/disk: (1/2) m R^2. Thin rod about end: (1/3) m L^2; about center: (1/12) m L^2. AP FRQs (Science Practice 1: Modeling) require students to identify the rotation axis before selecting the formula. Larger I means slower angular acceleration for the same applied torque; lower I/(mR^2) ratio wins rolling-down-incline races.

tau_net = I alpha — rotational form of Newton's second law (Euler's mid-18th-century reformulation). Net external torque equals moment of inertia times angular acceleration (rad/s^2). On the AP equation sheet. AP FRQs (Science Practice 5: Calculations) chain: compute torques (tau = r F sin theta), sum for tau_net, divide by I to find alpha. Equilibrium requires tau_net = 0 in addition to F_net = 0. Limiting case: tau_net = 0 gives alpha = 0 (constant omega or rest — rotational analog of Newton's first law). Combined with linear F = m a for problems with translation + rotation.

Angular momentum L = I omega is conserved when net external torque on the system is zero (tau_ext = 0). Internal torques cancel pairwise by Newton's third law extended to rotation. AP FRQs (Science Practice 5: Calculations) apply: I_i omega_i = I_f omega_f. Famous applications: figure skater pulling arms in (I decreases, omega increases); pulsar formation (collapsing star's I drops by factor 10^10, omega rises to ms period); Kepler's second law for planets (equal areas swept in equal times). Paragraph-length arguments (Science Practice 7) must explicitly justify 'tau_ext = 0' before invoking conservation.

Figure skater spin-up: pulling arms inward reduces I (mass closer to spin axis) by factor 2-4; since L = I omega must stay constant (no external torque), omega increases proportionally. Quantitative: I_i = 5 kg*m^2, omega_i = 2 rad/s → L = 10. If I_f = 2, then omega_f = 5 rad/s. AP FRQs (Science Practice 6: Argumentation) require students to explain that KE_rot = (1/2) I omega^2 INCREASES from 10 J to 25 J — the skater does +15 J of work pulling against centripetal forces of arms. Energy is NOT conserved; it's supplied by chemical energy in muscles. L is conserved; KE is not.

Hooke's Law: F = -k x (newtons), where k is spring constant (N/m) and x is displacement from equilibrium (m). Discovered by Robert Hooke (1660s). Negative sign indicates restoring direction (toward equilibrium). On the AP equation sheet. Linear in x — valid within elastic limit. Newton's second law with this force gives SHM: m a = -k x, with solutions x(t) = A cos(omega t + phi) where omega = sqrt(k/m). AP FRQs (Science Practice 5: Calculations) chain Hooke's Law with energy conservation: (1/2) k x_0^2 = (1/2) m v_max^2 at equilibrium. Pendulum analog (small-angle): F = -m g (x/L).

Mass-on-spring period: T = 2 pi sqrt(m/k) (seconds). On the AP equation sheet. Independent of amplitude (Galilean isochronism). Derived from omega = sqrt(k/m). AP FRQs (Science Practice 5: Calculations): a 0.5 kg mass on a 200 N/m spring has T = 2 pi sqrt(0.5/200) ≈ 0.314 s. Limiting cases (Science Practice 6: Argumentation): stiffer spring (larger k) gives shorter T (faster oscillation); heavier mass (larger m) gives longer T (slower). Experimental-design FRQs (Science Practice 4) plot T^2 vs m to extract k = 4 pi^2 / slope.

Small-angle pendulum period: T = 2 pi sqrt(L/g) (seconds). On the AP equation sheet. Independent of mass (Galilean equivalence principle) and amplitude (small-angle approximation). For L = 1 m on Earth (g = 9.8): T ≈ 2.0 s — the 'seconds pendulum.' On the moon (g/6): same pendulum has T ≈ 4.9 s. Galileo discovered isochronism c.1602 by timing chandelier swings against his pulse; Huygens (1656) built the first pendulum clock. AP FRQs (Science Practice 4: Experimental Design) often ask students to verify T = 2 pi sqrt(L/g) by varying L and timing T.

Amplitude A (meters) is the maximum displacement from equilibrium in SHM. The oscillator's position varies between +A and -A. Total energy E_total = (1/2) k A^2 — quadratic in A, so doubling amplitude quadruples energy. v_max = A omega = A sqrt(k/m) reached at equilibrium. T and omega are INDEPENDENT of A for ideal SHM (Galilean isochronism). AP FRQs (Science Practice 6: Argumentation) test this isochronism: doubling A doubles v_max but does NOT change T (since omega = sqrt(k/m) does not contain A). Important practical limit: real systems are isochronous only for small amplitudes; large amplitudes have nonlinear corrections.

Natural (resonant) frequency f_0 = (1/(2 pi)) sqrt(k_eff / m_eff) (Hz). The frequency at which a system oscillates when displaced from equilibrium and released. When external driving frequency f_drive = f_0, resonance occurs — amplitude grows dramatically, limited only by damping. AP FRQs (Science Practice 6: Argumentation) classic: Tacoma Narrows Bridge collapse (1940) — wind-induced oscillations matched torsional natural frequency. Other examples: pushing a child on a swing in time with each swing; shattering wine glass with a sung tone matching its natural mode; LC radio-receiver circuits tuning to station frequencies.

Density rho = m / V (kg/m^3) — mass per unit volume. Water: rho = 1000 kg/m^3. Air at sea level: rho = 1.225 kg/m^3. Mercury: rho = 13,600 kg/m^3. AP FRQs (Science Practice 5: Calculations) chain density with buoyancy (F_b = rho_fluid V_displaced g) and pressure (P = rho g h). Density depends on temperature (water expands when heated) and pressure (negligible for liquids in AP problems; large for gases — relevant in AP Physics 2 thermodynamics).

Pressure P = F / A (Pa = N/m^2), force perpendicular to a surface divided by contact area. Pascal (1647) gave name to the SI unit. Scalar in static fluids (acts equally in all directions at a point) — Pascal's principle. AP FRQs (Science Practice 5: Calculations) compute pressure at depth (P = P_atm + rho g h), on submarine windows (multi-atmosphere magnitudes at 100+ m depth), and on airplane wings (Bernoulli effect creates pressure differences that produce lift). Atmospheric pressure at sea level: 1.013 * 10^5 Pa = 1 atm.

Buoyancy F_b is the upward force on any submerged object from pressure differences between deeper (high P) and shallower (low P) parts. Archimedes' principle (c. 250 BCE): F_b = rho_fluid V_displaced g (newtons). On the AP equation sheet. AP FRQs (Science Practice 5: Calculations) compare F_b with weight F_g to determine sinking/floating: F_b > F_g → upward acceleration; F_b = F_g → floating equilibrium (V_disp / V_object = rho_object / rho_fluid). Iceberg in water: rho_ice / rho_water ≈ 0.917, so ~92% submerged.

Pascal's principle (1647): pressure applied at one point in a confined incompressible fluid is transmitted undiminished to every other point. Blaise Pascal demonstrated this experimentally. Foundation of hydraulic systems. AP FRQs (Science Practice 5: Calculations) use Pascal's principle in hydraulic-press problems: a small piston (A_1) under force F_1 produces pressure P = F_1/A_1, transmitted to a large piston (A_2 >> A_1) where F_2 = P A_2 = F_1 (A_2/A_1) — mechanical advantage A_2/A_1. Used in car jacks and braking systems. Distance trade-off (V conservation): small piston moves A_2/A_1 times farther.

Bernoulli's equation: P + (1/2) rho v^2 + rho g h = constant along a streamline (Pa) for incompressible inviscid steady flow. Daniel Bernoulli (1738) derived it from energy conservation applied to a fluid element. On the AP equation sheet. Three energy-density forms: pressure P, kinetic (1/2) rho v^2, gravitational rho g h. AP FRQs (Science Practice 5: Calculations) chain Bernoulli with continuity (A v = constant) for narrowing pipes, airplane wing lift (faster flow above → lower P → upward force), and water-tower flow rates. Bernoulli effect: faster flow → lower pressure (same streamline, same height).
